## Enforcement
Violations may result in:
- Immediate revocation of access
- Security incident review
- Required retraining
- Disciplinary action up to termination (per company policy)

## Change management
- Policy changes require review by Finance (RevRec), Security, and Legal.
- Metering schema changes that impact billing must follow the metering change control process (shadow billing compare period, sign-off, and audit evidence).
- The document owner must re-review this policy at least **annually** or after any major billing pipeline redesign.

## Owners
- **Policy owner (Finance/Legal):** Emily Stone (RevRec)
- **Telemetry owner:** Logan Wright
- **Security owner:** Security & Compliance on-call rotation (reviewed by Aisha Bello)

## Changelog
- 2026-02-22: Clarified retention schedule for usage events vs billing ledger; added explicit audit log retention and quarterly recertification.
- 2026-01-18: Added column-level restriction guidance for pricing/credits; clarified allowed purposes for Product Ops.
- 2025-11-12: Initial publication.

source.title: Rights-to-Spend-and-Access-Governance-Manual
source.author: Maya Thompson
source.content:
Executive summary

This manual consolidates Redwood Inference company-wide policies for access and permissions, change management, data handling, procurement & expenses, vendor lifecycle, travel policy, and the core internal templates used for approvals. It defines approval thresholds, role ownership, compliance checkpoints, and repeatable procedures so teams can operate consistently while minimizing security and financial risk.

Scope

Applies to all Redwood employees, contractors, and suppliers that access company systems, request purchases or vendor engagements, or process Redwood data. Exceptions must be approved in writing by Finance + Security + Legal.

Principles

- Least privilege: Grant the minimum permissions required.
- Separation of duties: Approvals must cross organizational boundaries where material risk exists.
- Clear thresholds: Dollar and access thresholds determine routing and audit needs.
- Time-boxed reviews: Access and vendor relationships are reviewed on a regular cadence.
- Traceability: All requests and approvals must be recorded in the company system of record (ProcureNow / AccessFlow).

1) Roles and responsibilities

- Requester: Initiates requests (access, purchase, travel). Responsible for providing justification and business owner sign-off.
- Manager: Verifies need and budget alignment for requests up to $5,000; completes initial review.
- Department Head: Approves requests between $5,001 and $50,000.
- Procurement Owner: Manages vendor selection, contract intake, and PO issuance.
- Finance Reviewer: Validates budget coding, pays invoices, and enforces expense policy.
- Security Reviewer: Reviews vendor security posture and high‑risk access requests (Tier 3 privileged access).
- Legal: Reviews contracts > $50,000, data processing agreements, and export controls.
- Access Approver (IAM): Final approval for role-based access changes and privileged access.

2) Access & permissions process

Access tiers

- Tier 0 — Public: No authentication required for public docs.
- Tier 1 — Standard: Day-to-day product and internal tools; manager + IAM auto-approve for standard groups.
- Tier 2 — Sensitive: PII, customer data, or financial systems; requires manager + Security Reviewer + IAM.
- Tier 3 — Privileged: Admin, infra, crypto keys; requires manager + Security + IAM + quarterly audit enrollment.

Request workflow (typical)

1. Requester completes Access Request Template in AccessFlow, including justification, least-privilege mapping, and expiration time.
2. Manager reviews and either approves or requests additional info within 48 hours.
3. Requests for Tier 2/3 are routed automatically to Security for a technical review (SLA: 72 hours).
4. IAM implements the change and records the workflow ID.
5. Access expiry is set at request time; a periodic access certification runs every 90 days for Tier 2 and every 30 days for Tier 3.

Access review & recertification

- Quarterly access recertification: Team managers receive a recertification report and must confirm, remove, or escalate access for each member.
- Automated reminders at 30/14/3 days before expiration.

Emergency access (break glass)

- Defined break-glass accounts require a post-facto audit: emergency justification must be logged within 24 hours, Security must perform a forensic check within 72 hours, and the access is revoked immediately when the emergency is resolved.

3) Change management

Change categories

- Standard change: Pre-approved, low-risk (e.g., routine backups, minor config changes). No CAB if schedule adheres to standard window.
- Major change: Architecture changes, database migrations, release candidate changes with potential user impact. Requires Change Advisory Board (CAB) approval and scheduled maintenance window.
- Emergency change: Remedial fix for incidents. Documented in incident system and reviewed retrospectively by CAB.

Change approval matrix

- Low risk (<$1,000 operational impact; no customer-facing impact): Team lead approval.
- Medium risk ($1,000–$50,000 or potential limited customer impact): Platform manager + SRE lead.
- High risk (> $50,000 impact or high customer impact): Engineering director + Product + Security + Change Board.

Process steps for Major change

1. Submit a Change Request in ChangeTracker with scope, rollback plan, test plan, and impact analysis at least 7 business days prior to the intended window.
2. Pre-deploy QA in staging and a dress rehearsal within the safety sandbox.
3. CAB review: present risk/opportunity, mitigation, and rollback plan. CAB decisions are recorded and distributed.
4. Execute in the approved window, with an on-call incident responder and a dedicated rollback runbook.
5. Post-deploy review within 48 hours documenting metrics, incidents, and lessons learned.

4) Data management and classification

Classification levels

- Public: Data safe for public release.
- Internal: Company operational data not meant for public release.
- Confidential: PII, customer data, contracts. Requires controls for access and encryption at rest and in transit.
- Restricted: Regulated data or material subject to additional contractual obligations (e.g., HIPAA, certain customer regulated workloads). Requires Data Protection Agreement and special onboarding.

Retention and deletion

- Default retention for Internal data: 3 years.
- Confidential: 7 years or per contractual requirement.
- Restricted: As defined in the contract and regulatory guidance; retention schedules must be documented in the Data Inventory.

Encryption & key management

- All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest and in transit. Customer-managed keys are supported for Private deployments. Keys for hosted metadata are rotated quarterly.

Backups and recovery

- Critical production datasets: RPO <= 1 hour, RTO <= 4 hours. Backups tested semi‑annually with documented results.

Data access when engaging vendors

- Vendors must only receive the minimal dataset required. Data sharing requires a signed DPA and a security assessment (see Vendor Security Checklist template).

5) Procurement and expenses

Approval thresholds (examples)

- Expense <= $500: Manager self-approve (receipts required in ExpenseNow).
- $501–$5,000: Manager + Finance approver.
- $5,001–$50,000: Department Head + Procurement + Finance.
- > $50,000: VP-level approval + Procurement + Legal for contracts.

Preferred vendor program

- Procurement maintains a list of vetted vendors with negotiated terms. Use preferred vendors whenever feasible to leverage volume discounts and pre-existing DPAs.

Purchase order (PO) process

1. Create Purchase Request in ProcureNow with line items, budget code, and vendor info.
2. Procurement validates vendor, checks preferred list, and issues a PO upon approval.
3. Vendor invoices against the PO; Finance performs 3-way match where applicable.

Expense reimbursements

- Employees must submit expense reports within 30 days. Personal travel that exceeds policy thresholds requires pre-approval. Per diem rates are published on the Travel Templates page.

6) Vendor management lifecycle

Onboarding checklist (minimum)

- Business case and owner identified.
- Vendor security questionnaire completed and reviewed by Security.
- Contract and DPA signed if data sharing is required.
- PO and payment terms agreed.
- Account provisioning limited to necessary permissions and monitored.

Risk tiers and cadence

- Low risk: Annual review.
- Medium risk: Bi-annual review with spot audits.
- High risk (hosts customer data or core infra): Quarterly review and contractual audit rights.

Exit and offboarding

- Revoke vendor accounts immediately after contract termination.
- Verify data deletion or return per contract; document certificate of destruction where applicable.

7) Corporate travel policy (concise reference)

Booking and pre-approval

- All travel must use the company travel portal. International travel requires manager + People Ops pre-approval; high-risk geographies require Security notification.

Air travel and class

- Domestic flights: Economy. Exceptions for flights >6 hours subject to manager approval.
- International flights: Economy for travel <8 hours; Premium Economy may be approved by Manager for >8 hours.

Lodging and per diem

- Reasonable business-class hotels; nightly cap published on Travel Templates. Meals reimbursed per diem or itemized receipts; personal entertainment is non-reimbursable.

Expense reconciliation

- Submit itemized receipts within 15 days of trip. International currency conversions use the Finance-approved rate.

Safety and security

- Employees must complete the Traveler Safety briefing when traveling to locations flagged on the Redwood travel risk map. Emergency contact info must be entered into the travel portal prior to departure.

8) Templates and forms (included samples)

Access Request Template (example fields)

- requester_name: "Jane Doe"
- team: "Platform"
- environment: "prod"
- resource: "s3://customer-logs"
- access_tier: "Tier 2"
- justification: "Required for troubleshooting security alert #2026-77"
- expiration_date: "2026-03-01"
- manager_approval: "true"

Purchase Request Template (example)

- requester_name: "Alex Kim"
- vendor: "Acme Cloud Storage"
- amount: "12500"
- budget_code: "ENG-CAPEX-2026"
- justification: "Increased object storage for log retention"
- procurement_owner: "procurement@redwood.ai"

Vendor Security Checklist (selection)

- Does the vendor encrypt data at rest and in transit? (yes/no)
- SOC 2 or equivalent evidence provided? (attach)
- Subprocessor list provided and approved? (yes/no)
- Incident reporting SLA: <24 hours required for high-risk vendors

9) Audit, evidence, and reporting

- All approvals, POs, contracts, and access logs must be retained per the Data Retention schedule and are subject to audit.
- Quarterly governance report: owner_team consolidates exceptions, high-severity vendor findings, and spend over thresholds for the Executive Risk Committee.

10) Exceptions and escalation

- Exceptions must be requested via ExceptionRequest and include business justification, compensating controls, owner, and expiry. Exceptions require sign-off from Finance + Security + Legal as appropriate.

Appendix A — Definitions

- PO: Purchase order.
- DPA: Data processing agreement.
- RPO / RTO: Recovery point / time objectives.
- CAB: Change Advisory Board.

Appendix B — Revision history

- 2025-11-03: Drafted by Maya Thompson.
- 2026-01-21: Updated procurement thresholds and travel per diem.
- 2026-02-14: Published after cross-functional review (Finance, Security, Legal).

Related resources

- Procurement system (ProcureNow): /internal/procurenow
- Access system (AccessFlow): /internal/accessflow
- Change tracker: /internal/changetracker

Contact

- Finance: finance-ops@redwood.ai
- Procurement: procurement@redwood.ai
- Security: secops@redwood.ai
- Travel & People Ops: travel@redwood.ai

End of document

source.title: Exception Policy: Optimize Quality Gate Overrides
source.author: Marcus Lin
related_pages: ADR-0231 Quality gates required for Optimize config changes
Quality gate metrics and thresholds v1
source.body:
## Summary
Optimize can recommend and apply cost-saving configuration changes (batching policy, prefix/KV caching settings, and quantization profiles). Because these changes can materially affect model outputs, **quality gates are mandatory** prior to production rollout.

This policy defines the **only approved path to override** a failing or missing quality gate, including who can approve, time bounds, required evidence, audit requirements, and required follow-up.

Overrides are intended to be **rare, time-bound, and traceable**. The default expectation is to resolve the underlying issue (regression, flakiness, or infra failure) rather than bypass gating.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Overrides for Optimize-proposed configuration changes:
  - Batching policy updates
  - Prefix/KV caching settings
  - Quantization profile changes (per model family / hardware SKU)
- Overrides that affect:
  - Hosted API production traffic
  - Dedicated clusters managed by Redwood
  - Staging environments when they are used as release qualification for production

### Out of scope
- Private/on-prem customer-managed deployments where Redwood does not control enforcement (handled via documented manual workflow; see “Private workflow” note below).
- Model upgrades or prompt/template changes (covered by separate release management policies).

---

## Definitions
- **Gate run:** An evaluation execution initiated by Optimize using eval-harness against a baseline, producing a pass/fail decision and artifacts.
- **Gate failure:** A gate run that fails regression thresholds, times out beyond configured limits, or is deemed invalid due to flakiness/quarantine.
- **Override:** A time-bound permission that allows a config change to proceed despite a failing or missing gate.
- **Break-glass override:** Emergency override used to mitigate a live incident (Sev0/Sev1) when gating infrastructure is unavailable or when immediate action is required.

---

## Policy statement
1) **Quality gates are required** for applying Optimize configuration changes to production traffic.
2) **Overrides are permitted only** under the conditions and approval requirements in this document.
3) **All overrides must be time-bound** and must include an owner, reason, evidence, and a follow-up plan.
4) **Defense-in-depth enforcement:** Even if an override is granted, standard rollout safeguards remain in place (canary + automatic rollback hooks, where supported).

---

## Roles and approval authority
### Who can request an override
Any Redwood employee with access to Optimize recommendations for the relevant account/route may request an override, typically:
- Optimize oncall engineer
- Account owner / solutions engineer (for enterprise customers)
- Product oncall (Optimize)

### Who can approve an override
Approvals are based on **risk tier** and **blast radius**.

**Approver groups (by function):**
- Product: Aditya Rao (Optimize)
- Applied ML / Evals: Karthik Iyer or Jonas Weber
- SRE: Sean Gallagher (or designated SRE oncall)
- Runtime safety: Noah Patel or Mateo Alvarez
- Security (audit/RBAC): Marcus Lin (required only for customer-sensitive or policy exceptions; see below)

### Minimum approver set
| Change / Situation | Minimum approvers required |
|---|---|
| Non-production (dev/staging) | 1 approver: owning eng lead (Optimize or Runtime) |
| Production, limited blast radius (single route, canary-only) | 2 approvers: (Applied ML/Evals) + (SRE or Runtime safety) |
| Production, broad blast radius (multi-route, default policy change, auto-apply enablement) | 3 approvers: (Applied ML/Evals) + (SRE) + (Product lead) |
| Any override that disables audit logging, weakens RBAC, or alters retention of gate artifacts | 3 approvers + **Security** |
| Break-glass during Sev0/Sev1 | 1 approver: SRE oncall or Runtime safety oncall, with after-the-fact ratification (see below) |

Notes:
- The **Console “Override Approver” RBAC role** is required to approve. Approval authority is granted via internal support request and reviewed quarterly.
- Approval cannot be self-approved by the requester.

---

## Allowed override reasons (and disallowed reasons)
### Allowed reasons
An override request must select one primary reason code:
1) **False positive due to confirmed eval flakiness**
   - Evidence: flake score above threshold and/or prompt set quarantined; rerun(s) show inconsistent outcomes.
2) **Gate infrastructure failure** (eval-harness outage, GPU capacity incident, region-specific timeouts)
   - Evidence: incident link or error logs; retries exhausted per contract.
3) **Urgent customer/business need** with bounded risk
   - Example: time-sensitive launch with constrained canary traffic and strong online monitoring.
4) **Safety mitigation**
   - Example: change reduces error rate/latency to restore service and is expected to be quality-neutral or quality-positive.

### Disallowed reasons
Overrides are not permitted for:
- Avoiding evaluation cost or time when gates are healthy.
- Applying changes to “see what happens” without a rollback plan.
- Proceeding when a gate failure indicates a clear quality regression without a mitigation strategy.

---

## Time bounds and constraints
All overrides must specify an expiration time.

Default and maximum durations:
- **Standard override:** default 24 hours; maximum 72 hours.
- **Extended override:** up to 7 days, requires additional approval from an executive engineering owner (Priya Natarajan or Mateo Alvarez) and a written follow-up plan.
- **Break-glass override:** maximum 12 hours; must be ratified within 24 hours by the minimum approver set for the blast radius.

Hard constraints:
- Overrides **must be scoped** to the smallest viable unit:
  - Specific route(s)
  - Specific model version(s)
  - Specific hardware SKU / quant profile
  - Canary-only where feasible
- Overrides **must not** allow “full rollout” unless explicitly approved under the “broad blast radius” row above.

---

## Required evidence checklist
An override request is incomplete without the following attachments/links:

1) **Config diff details**
- Proposed change type (batching / caching / quantization)
- Before/after values
- Affected model family, hardware SKU, and route(s)

2) **Gate run references** (when a run exists)
- Gate run ID(s)
- Artifact links (eval report, metrics summary, failure cases)
- Comparison to baseline (delta metrics)

3) **Reason-specific evidence**
- Flakiness: flake score, rerun results, quarantine record (if applicable)
- Infra: incident ticket or status page link, retry logs, region
- Business need: customer impact statement and deadline

4) **Risk assessment (required for production)**
- Expected quality impact and why
- Blast radius + exposure controls (canary %, account scope)
- Rollback plan and rollback trigger thresholds
- Online monitoring plan (dashboards/alerts to watch)

5) **Owner and follow-up plan**
- Single accountable owner (name)
- Concrete follow-up tasks with due dates (see below)

---

## Approval workflow (Console-first)
### Standard flow
1) Requester clicks **“Request override”** in Console from the gate results panel.
2) Requester completes the override form (reason, scope, expiration, evidence links).
3) Console routes the request to the required approver set based on blast radius.
4) Approvers review:
   - Gate artifacts
   - Risk controls (scope/canary)
   - Rollback triggers and monitoring
5) If approved, Console issues an **override grant** tied to:
   - gate run ID (or “no gate run available” + infra reason)
   - config diff hash
   - scope and expiration
6) Apply and rollout proceed under the granted scope.

### Break-glass flow (Sev0/Sev1)
1) Oncall declares break-glass in the incident channel and opens an incident ticket.
2) Approver grants break-glass override with **12-hour expiration** and strict scope.
3) After stabilization, within 24 hours:
   - rerun gates (or equivalent evaluation)
   - complete ratification with the standard minimum approver set
   - file follow-up tasks

---

## Audit and logging requirements
### Mandatory audit fields
Every override must be recorded with:
- Override ID
- Requester identity
- Approver identity (all approvers)
- Timestamp of request and approval
- Expiration timestamp
- Scope (routes, models, SKU, environment)
- Config diff hash and human-readable diff summary
- Linked gate run ID(s) and artifact pointers (or infra failure evidence)
- Reason code + free-text justification
- Follow-up ticket links

### Audit event emission
Systems must emit audit events for:
- `quality_gate.override_requested`
- `quality_gate.override_granted`
- `quality_gate.override_denied`
- `quality_gate.override_expired`
- `quality_gate.override_revoked`

Audit events must be queryable by:
- account
- route
- model family
- config change type
- requester/approver

Retention:
- Audit events: minimum **1 year**.
- Gate artifacts referenced by an override: minimum **90 days** (or longer per enterprise retention policy).

---

## Post-override follow-up (required)
An override is not complete until follow-up is filed and tracked.

### Required follow-up tasks (minimum)
Within **2 business days** of the override grant (or next business day for break-glass):
1) Create a tracking ticket (Linear/Jira) titled: “Follow-up: Quality gate override <Override ID>”.
2) Attach:
   - override details
   - gate artifacts
   - rollout outcome (canary metrics)
3) Complete at least one of:
   - Fix the underlying issue (eval flakiness, infra timeout, prompt set gap)
   - Adjust thresholds/metrics via the formal metrics change process
   - Re-run gates with stabilized conditions and remove the override

### Additional required tasks by reason
- Flakiness: quarantine prompt set or mark flaky suite; assign to Applied ML (Jonas Weber / Karthik Iyer) for remediation.
- Infra failure: open SRE follow-up; ensure regional capacity/timeout issue is addressed; link incident postmortem if applicable.
- Business need: schedule a retro within 7 days to determine why gates were blocking and whether additional fast gates or coverage are needed.

### Override revocation
Approvers (or SRE oncall) may revoke an active override if:
- online quality signals regress beyond rollback thresholds
- scope is violated (e.g., applied to additional routes)
- new evidence indicates the original reason was invalid

---

## Enforcement and compliance
- Applying an Optimize config change to production without required gates or a valid override is a **policy violation**.
- Repeated violations are escalated to the owning director (Platform or Runtime) and Security for review of access permissions.
- The “Override Approver” RBAC group membership is reviewed quarterly; access is removed when no longer required.

---

## Private workflow note (manual)
For Private/on-prem customers where automated enforcement is not yet delivered:
- Exceptions must be documented in the customer change record with the same evidence checklist.
- Approvals must be captured via ticket + written sign-off from the minimum approver set for the blast radius.
- Customers must receive a written explanation of residual risk and rollback steps.

---

## Appendix A: Override request template (for ticket-based workflows)
Include the following in the request:
- Requester:
- Environment:
- Account / route(s):
- Model family + version:
- Hardware SKU:
- Change type (batching/caching/quant):
- Config diff (before/after):
- Gate run IDs + artifact links:
- Reason code:
- Risk controls (canary %, rollout steps):
- Rollback plan + triggers:
- Expiration time:
- Follow-up owner + tasks:

---

## Appendix B: Rollback triggers (recommended defaults)
Use the stricter of customer SLOs or the following:
- Online quality proxy drop beyond agreed threshold for 2 consecutive windows
- Error rate increase beyond normal band for 10 minutes
- Latency p95 regression beyond rollout policy threshold
- Any customer-reported correctness issue attributable to the change

(See: Runbook: Optimize quality gate failures; Quality gates observability standard.)

- Gate run ID(s)
- Artifact links (eval report, metrics summary, failure cases)
- Comparison to baseline (delta metrics)

3) **Reason-specific evidence**
- Flakiness: flake score, rerun results, quarantine record (if applicable)
- Infra: incident ticket or status page link, retry logs, region
- Business need: customer impact statement and deadline

4) **Risk assessment (required for production)**
- Expected quality impact and why
- Blast radius + exposure controls (canary %, account scope)
- Rollback plan and rollback trigger thresholds
- Online monitoring plan (dashboards/alerts to watch)

5) **Owner and follow-up plan**
- Single accountable owner (name)
- Concrete follow-up tasks with due dates (see below)

---

## Approval workflow (Console-first)
### Standard flow
1) Requester clicks **“Request override”** in Console from the gate results panel.
2) Requester completes the override form (reason, scope, expiration, evidence links).
3) Console routes the request to the required approver set based on blast radius.
4) Approvers review:
   - Gate artifacts
   - Risk controls (scope/canary)
   - Rollback triggers and monitoring
5) If approved, Console issues an **override grant** tied to:
   - gate run ID (or “no gate run available” + infra reason)
   - config diff hash
   - scope and expiration
6) Apply and rollout proceed under the granted scope.

### Break-glass flow (Sev0/Sev1)
1) Oncall declares break-glass in the incident channel and opens an incident ticket.
2) Approver grants break-glass override with **12-hour expiration** and strict scope.
3) After stabilization, within 24 hours:
   - rerun gates (or equivalent evaluation)
   - complete ratification with the standard minimum approver set
   - file follow-up tasks

---

## Audit and logging requirements
### Mandatory audit fields
Every override must be recorded with:
- Override ID
- Requester identity
- Approver identity (all approvers)
- Timestamp of request and approval
- Expiration timestamp
- Scope (routes, models, SKU, environment)
- Config diff hash and human-readable diff summary
- Linked gate run ID(s) and artifact pointers (or infra failure evidence)
- Reason code + free-text justification
- Follow-up ticket links

### Audit event emission
Systems must emit audit events for:
- `quality_gate.override_requested`
- `quality_gate.override_granted`
- `quality_gate.override_denied`
- `quality_gate.override_expired`
- `quality_gate.override_revoked`

Audit events must be queryable by:
- account
- route
- model family
- config change type
- requester/approver

Retention:
- Audit events: minimum **1 year**.
- Gate artifacts referenced by an override: minimum **90 days** (or longer per enterprise retention policy).

---

## Post-override follow-up (required)
An override is not complete until follow-up is filed and tracked.

### Required follow-up tasks (minimum)
Within **2 business days** of the override grant (or next business day for break-glass):
1) Create a tracking ticket (Linear/Jira) titled: “Follow-up: Quality gate override <Override ID>”.
2) Attach:
   - override details
   - gate artifacts
   - rollout outcome (canary metrics)
3) Complete at least one of:
   - Fix the underlying issue (eval flakiness, infra timeout, prompt set gap)
   - Adjust thresholds/metrics via the formal metrics change process
   - Re-run gates with stabilized conditions and remove the override

### Additional required tasks by reason
- Flakiness: quarantine prompt set or mark flaky suite; assign to Applied ML (Jonas Weber / Karthik Iyer) for remediation.
- Infra failure: open SRE follow-up; ensure regional capacity/timeout issue is addressed; link incident postmortem if applicable.
- Business need: schedule a retro within 7 days to determine why gates were blocking and whether additional fast gates or coverage are needed.

### Override revocation
Approvers (or SRE oncall) may revoke an active override if:
- online quality signals regress beyond rollback thresholds
- scope is violated (e.g., applied to additional routes)
- new evidence indicates the original reason was invalid

---

## Enforcement and compliance
- Applying an Optimize config change to production without required gates or a valid override is a **policy violation**.
- Repeated violations are escalated to the owning director (Platform or Runtime) and Security for review of access permissions.
- The “Override Approver” RBAC group membership is reviewed quarterly; access is removed when no longer required.

---

## Private workflow note (manual)
For Private/on-prem customers where automated enforcement is not yet delivered:
- Exceptions must be documented in the customer change record with the same evidence checklist.
- Approvals must be captured via ticket + written sign-off from the minimum approver set for the blast radius.
- Customers must receive a written explanation of residual risk and rollback steps.

---

## Appendix A: Override request template (for ticket-based workflows)
Include the following in the request:
- Requester:
- Environment:
- Account / route(s):
- Model family + version:
- Hardware SKU:
- Change type (batching/caching/quant):
- Config diff (before/after):
- Gate run IDs + artifact links:
- Reason code:
- Risk controls (canary %, rollout steps):
- Rollback plan + triggers:
- Expiration time:
- Follow-up owner + tasks:

---

## Appendix B: Rollback triggers (recommended defaults)
Use the stricter of customer SLOs or the following:
- Online quality proxy drop beyond agreed threshold for 2 consecutive windows
- Error rate increase beyond normal band for 10 minutes
- Latency p95 regression beyond rollout policy threshold
- Any customer-reported correctness issue attributable to the change

(See: Runbook: Optimize quality gate failures; Quality gates observability standard.)
links: ADR-0231 Quality gates required for Optimize config changes
Quality gate metrics and thresholds v1

source.title: Production Optimization Change Policy (Safe Apply + Rollback)
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.content:
## Purpose
This policy defines the required process, reviewers, canary strategy, and documentation expectations for **production optimization changes** on Redwood Inference.

Optimization changes include configuration updates that affect inference economics or performance characteristics (e.g., batching heuristics, caching toggles, quantization profiles). Because these changes can impact latency, error rates, quality, or customer workloads, they must be applied safely, be auditable, and be reversible.

This policy exists to:
- Reduce change-failure risk and customer impact.
- Ensure every change is traceable (who/what/when/why).
- Ensure a known-good rollback path is available before widening blast radius.

## Scope
This policy applies to production changes that alter **Optimization Config** behavior for any of:
- **Hosted API** (Redwood-managed)
- **Dedicated** capacity (Redwood-managed)
- **Private** deployments (customer-managed control plane; Redwood-provided tooling/operator guidance)

In scope examples:
- Batching controller configuration (max batch size, max delay, shard rules)
- Prefix/KV caching enablement/disablement and cache sizing limits
- Quantization profile selection or parameters (including model-specific profiles)
- Runtime routing policies that are part of the Optimization Config surface

Out of scope:
- Code changes shipped via runtime release train (covered by the standard Release Train Policy)
- Changes unrelated to inference optimization (e.g., billing configuration, authentication settings)

## Definitions
- **Optimization Config Version**: An immutable, versioned configuration object stored in the control plane.
- **Safe Apply**: A staged rollout process that validates configuration, applies canaries, and gates progression on health/quality signals.
- **Promotion**: Advancing an applied version to a wider scope (e.g., from canary to 100%, or from one region to multiple).
- **Rollback**: Reverting an active scope to a prior known-good version.
- **Blast radius**: The maximum expected customer and capacity impact if the change performs poorly.

## Policy statement (non-negotiables)
1) **All production optimization changes MUST be performed via the Optimization Config Service safe-apply workflow.**
   - No direct edits to live mutable configuration are allowed.
   - All changes must produce an immutable version and an audit trail.

2) **Every change MUST have an identified rollback target and rollback procedure before apply.**
   - If there is no known-good version to revert to (or rollback cannot be executed safely), the change cannot proceed.

3) **Progressive delivery is required.**
   - Canary-first, gated promotion, and explicit approval for high-risk changes.

4) **Audit logging and RBAC are mandatory.**
   - Actions (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) must emit the required audit events.
   - Only users with the appropriate role (Optimization Change Operator / Approver) may apply or rollback.

## Roles and responsibilities
- **Change Author** (typically Platform Control Plane or Runtime engineer)
  - Creates the config version, writes documentation, proposes rollout scope, and monitors canary.
- **Optimization Change Operator**
  - Executes safe apply/promotion/rollback in Console or API. Must be on-call trained for this workflow.
- **Approver**
  - Reviews risk and documentation; required for medium/high blast radius (see matrix below).
- **SRE On-Call**
  - Has authority to pause or rollback if gates fail or customer impact is detected.
- **Applied ML/Evals** (quantization only)
  - Confirms evaluation coverage and thresholds; reviews quality regressions and approves quant profile promotions.

## Blast radius classification
Classify blast radius based on the **largest impacted scope** during the rollout.

### Blast radius levels
- **Low**: Single tenant OR single model within one region; canary impact limited and reversible.
- **Medium**: Multiple tenants OR a tier/segment in one region; potential for noticeable aggregate impact.
- **High**: Cross-region, default/global settings, or changes that affect a large share of production traffic.

### Classification criteria (use the highest applicable)
- Scope size: number of tenants, models, regions
- Customer tier: enterprise/SLA customers included?
- Change type: cache disablement, batching delays, quantization profile changes
- Reversibility: rollback speed, expected cache invalidation effects
- Observability: are required metrics and dashboards available for this scope?

## Required reviewers and approvals
All changes require at least one reviewer; higher blast radius requires designated approvals.

### Reviewer/approval matrix
**Low blast radius**
- Required reviewer(s): 1 from Platform Control Plane OR Serving Runtime (not the author)
- Additional reviewer(s): optional SRE
- Approval: reviewer sign-off in the change record

**Medium blast radius**
- Required reviewer(s):
  - 1 from Platform Control Plane OR Serving Runtime (not the author)
  - 1 SRE (or SRE delegate)
- Quantization changes: +1 from Applied ML/Evals
- Approval: explicit approval in the change record before promotion beyond canary

**High blast radius**
- Required reviewer(s):
  - 1 from Platform Control Plane leadership (EM/tech lead)
  - 1 from Serving Runtime leadership (EM/tech lead)
  - 1 SRE lead or on-call designee
- Quantization changes: +Applied ML/Evals lead approval
- Security/audit-sensitive customers included: +Security reviewer (Product Security or Security PM)
- Approval: explicit approvals required before any production apply; promotions require reconfirmation after canary results

## Canary and rollout requirements
### Standard safe apply stages
1) **Validate**
   - Schema validation
   - Semantic validation (e.g., batching bounds, cache compatibility, quant profile constraints)
2) **Canary apply**
   - Apply to a limited scope/percentage
   - Monitor gates for the required duration
3) **Promote**
   - Expand scope gradually
   - Re-check gates at each step
4) **Complete**
   - Mark rollout complete and record final metrics snapshot

### Canary requirements by blast radius
**Low**
- Canary scope: at least 1 tenant/model/region shard; OR 1–5% of traffic for that scope
- Minimum duration: 30 minutes of representative traffic
- Required gates:
  - 5xx error rate: no sustained regression beyond threshold
  - p95 latency: no sustained regression beyond threshold

**Medium**
- Canary scope: 5–10% for at least one region OR one enterprise tenant canary (with consent)
- Minimum duration: 60–120 minutes (must include peak-ish traffic if available)
- Required gates:
  - 5xx error rate gate
  - p95 latency gate
  - p99 latency gate (recommended; required if change affects batching delay or cache settings)

**High**
- Canary scope: 1% → 5% → 10% progressive steps (at least 2 steps before reaching 25%+)
- Minimum duration: 2–6 hours total (or one full peak cycle where feasible)
- Required gates:
  - 5xx error rate gate
  - p95 and p99 latency gates
  - Saturation signals (GPU util/queue depth) must remain within safe limits
  - Explicit SRE acknowledgement before moving past 10% and before any cross-region expansion

### Additional requirements for quantization profile changes
- Must run eval harness gates as defined in the gating ADR.
- Canary must include:
  - A representative prompt set for the model(s)
  - Quality threshold checks (regression boundaries) AND false-positive analysis notes if signals are noisy
- Promotion requires Applied ML/Evals approval for medium/high blast radius.

### Gate thresholds (defaults)
Use current SLO toolkit defaults unless an exception is approved.
- 5xx error rate: do not exceed **baseline + 0.10%** sustained over 10 minutes for the canary scope
- p95 latency: do not exceed **baseline + 10%** sustained over 10 minutes for the canary scope
- p99 latency: do not exceed **baseline + 15%** sustained over 10 minutes for the canary scope

Notes:
- “Baseline” must be defined as the immediate pre-change period for the same scope, with the same tags.
- If baseline is unstable (incident ongoing), do not proceed.

### Pause and abort rules
- If any required gate fails:
  1) Rollout controller must **pause** automatically (if configured) or the operator must pause manually.
  2) Operator must notify #eng-sre and #eng-platform with the change link and scope.
  3) If customer impact is likely or metrics are deteriorating, **rollback** is preferred over prolonged investigation.

## Documentation requirements (change record)
A production optimization change is not compliant unless the following are recorded (Console change record or linked ticket):
- **Owner** and **on-call contact**
- **Config version IDs**: new version, prior version (rollback target)
- **Scope**: tenant(s), model(s), region(s), percentage plan
- **Blast radius classification** and justification
- **Diff summary**: what changed (include links to diff view)
- **Expected impact**: latency/cost/throughput hypothesis
- **Validation results**: schema/semantic validation status
- **Gating plan**: which gates, thresholds, and monitoring dashboards
- **Canary results**: timestamps, key metric snapshots, and explicit “proceed” decision
- **Rollback plan**: criteria to rollback and who is authorized to execute
- **Customer comms plan** (if enterprise/SLA customers impacted)

For quantization changes, additionally include:
- Eval harness run ID(s), prompt set(s), and pass/fail criteria
- Summary of quality deltas and any known tradeoffs

## Change windows and operational constraints
- Medium/high blast radius changes must be executed during staffed hours for the primary owning team and SRE (unless declared incident/emergency).
- Avoid starting high blast radius rollouts within 60 minutes of major runtime releases unless explicitly coordinated with Release Engineering.
- Cooldown: do not apply a second high blast radius optimization change to the same global scope within 24 hours unless incident-driven.

## Emergency change procedure (Sev response)
During a Sev incident, speed and customer protection take priority.

Emergency allowances:
- SRE On-Call may execute rollback without the full approval chain if gates fail or customer impact is present.
- Documentation may be minimal at time of action, but must be completed within **24 hours**.

Emergency requirements (still mandatory):
- Use the rollback workflow (Console/API) so audit events are emitted.
- Capture incident link and request IDs in the change record.

## Private deployments (customer-managed)
For Redwood Private environments:
- Customers/operators must pin an Optimization Config Version via supported tooling (installer/helm/terraform).
- Redwood recommends following the same blast radius and canary principles where feasible.
- Rollback must follow the operator guide and should be rehearsed during onboarding for regulated workloads.

Redwood support expectations:
- Support can guide customers through rollback procedures but must not bypass customer change controls.
- Where contractual SLAs require it, ensure audit log export is configured so rollback events are retained externally.

## Enforcement and exceptions
- RBAC enforces who can apply/promote/rollback. Requests for access must go through standard access review.
- Exceptions (e.g., skipping canary steps) require written approval from SRE lead and the owning engineering lead, and must be recorded with rationale.

## Appendix A: Minimal “good” change record template
Copy/paste into the change ticket/Console notes:
- Change name:
- Author / Operator:
- Service/scope (tenant/model/region):
- Blast radius: Low/Medium/High (reason):
- New config version:
- Rollback target version:
- Diff summary:
- Canary plan (%, duration, steps):
- Gates (thresholds):
- Dashboards:
- Canary results + decision:
- Rollout completion time:
- Rollback criteria:
- Approvals:

## Appendix B: Common failure modes (use as pre-flight checklist)
- Mis-scoped apply (wrong region or tenant)
- Cache invalidation causing transient p99 spikes
- Batching delay increases impacting interactive workloads
- Quantization profile regression not caught by eval coverage gaps
- Mixed worker state due to concurrent apply/rollback (ensure rollout controller state is healthy)

## Contact / ownership
- Policy owner: Release Engineering (Connor O'Brien)
- Operational owner for rollouts/rollback: SRE (Sean Gallagher)
- Security requirements (audit/RBAC): Security & Compliance (Naomi Feldman / Marcus Lin)

Questions or proposed changes to this policy should be filed as a ticket and reviewed by the owners above.

- Batching controller configuration (max batch size, max delay, shard rules)
- Prefix/KV caching enablement/disablement and cache sizing limits
- Quantization profile selection or parameters (including model-specific profiles)
- Runtime routing policies that are part of the Optimization Config surface

Out of scope:
- Code changes shipped via runtime release train (covered by the standard Release Train Policy)
- Changes unrelated to inference optimization (e.g., billing configuration, authentication settings)

## Definitions
- **Optimization Config Version**: An immutable, versioned configuration object stored in the control plane.
- **Safe Apply**: A staged rollout process that validates configuration, applies canaries, and gates progression on health/quality signals.
- **Promotion**: Advancing an applied version to a wider scope (e.g., from canary to 100%, or from one region to multiple).
- **Rollback**: Reverting an active scope to a prior known-good version.
- **Blast radius**: The maximum expected customer and capacity impact if the change performs poorly.

## Policy statement (non-negotiables)
1) **All production optimization changes MUST be performed via the Optimization Config Service safe-apply workflow.**
   - No direct edits to live mutable configuration are allowed.
   - All changes must produce an immutable version and an audit trail.

2) **Every change MUST have an identified rollback target and rollback procedure before apply.**
   - If there is no known-good version to revert to (or rollback cannot be executed safely), the change cannot proceed.

3) **Progressive delivery is required.**
   - Canary-first, gated promotion, and explicit approval for high-risk changes.

4) **Audit logging and RBAC are mandatory.**
   - Actions (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) must emit the required audit events.
   - Only users with the appropriate role (Optimization Change Operator / Approver) may apply or rollback.

## Roles and responsibilities
- **Change Author** (typically Platform Control Plane or Runtime engineer)
  - Creates the config version, writes documentation, proposes rollout scope, and monitors canary.
- **Optimization Change Operator**
  - Executes safe apply/promotion/rollback in Console or API. Must be on-call trained for this workflow.
- **Approver**
  - Reviews risk and documentation; required for medium/high blast radius (see matrix below).
- **SRE On-Call**
  - Has authority to pause or rollback if gates fail or customer impact is detected.
- **Applied ML/Evals** (quantization only)
  - Confirms evaluation coverage and thresholds; reviews quality regressions and approves quant profile promotions.

## Blast radius classification
Classify blast radius based on the **largest impacted scope** during the rollout.

### Blast radius levels
- **Low**: Single tenant OR single model within one region; canary impact limited and reversible.
- **Medium**: Multiple tenants OR a tier/segment in one region; potential for noticeable aggregate impact.
- **High**: Cross-region, default/global settings, or changes that affect a large share of production traffic.

### Classification criteria (use the highest applicable)
- Scope size: number of tenants, models, regions
- Customer tier: enterprise/SLA customers included?
- Change type: cache disablement, batching delays, quantization profile changes
- Reversibility: rollback speed, expected cache invalidation effects
- Observability: are required metrics and dashboards available for this scope?

## Required reviewers and approvals
All changes require at least one reviewer; higher blast radius requires designated approvals.

### Reviewer/approval matrix
**Low blast radius**
- Required reviewer(s): 1 from Platform Control Plane OR Serving Runtime (not the author)
- Additional reviewer(s): optional SRE
- Approval: reviewer sign-off in the change record

**Medium blast radius**
- Required reviewer(s):
  - 1 from Platform Control Plane OR Serving Runtime (not the author)
  - 1 SRE (or SRE delegate)
- Quantization changes: +1 from Applied ML/Evals
- Approval: explicit approval in the change record before promotion beyond canary

**High blast radius**
- Required reviewer(s):
  - 1 from Platform Control Plane leadership (EM/tech lead)
  - 1 from Serving Runtime leadership (EM/tech lead)
  - 1 SRE lead or on-call designee
- Quantization changes: +Applied ML/Evals lead approval
- Security/audit-sensitive customers included: +Security reviewer (Product Security or Security PM)
- Approval: explicit approvals required before any production apply; promotions require reconfirmation after canary results

## Canary and rollout requirements
### Standard safe apply stages
1) **Validate**
   - Schema validation
   - Semantic validation (e.g., batching bounds, cache compatibility, quant profile constraints)
2) **Canary apply**
   - Apply to a limited scope/percentage
   - Monitor gates for the required duration
3) **Promote**
   - Expand scope gradually
   - Re-check gates at each step
4) **Complete**
   - Mark rollout complete and record final metrics snapshot

### Canary requirements by blast radius
**Low**
- Canary scope: at least 1 tenant/model/region shard; OR 1–5% of traffic for that scope
- Minimum duration: 30 minutes of representative traffic
- Required gates:
  - 5xx error rate: no sustained regression beyond threshold
  - p95 latency: no sustained regression beyond threshold

**Medium**
- Canary scope: 5–10% for at least one region OR one enterprise tenant canary (with consent)
- Minimum duration: 60–120 minutes (must include peak-ish traffic if available)
- Required gates:
  - 5xx error rate gate
  - p95 latency gate
  - p99 latency gate (recommended; required if change affects batching delay or cache settings)

**High**
- Canary scope: 1% → 5% → 10% progressive steps (at least 2 steps before reaching 25%+)
- Minimum duration: 2–6 hours total (or one full peak cycle where feasible)
- Required gates:
  - 5xx error rate gate
  - p95 and p99 latency gates
  - Saturation signals (GPU util/queue depth) must remain within safe limits
  - Explicit SRE acknowledgement before moving past 10% and before any cross-region expansion

### Additional requirements for quantization profile changes
- Must run eval harness gates as defined in the gating ADR.
- Canary must include:
  - A representative prompt set for the model(s)
  - Quality threshold checks (regression boundaries) AND false-positive analysis notes if signals are noisy
- Promotion requires Applied ML/Evals approval for medium/high blast radius.

### Gate thresholds (defaults)
Use current SLO toolkit defaults unless an exception is approved.
- 5xx error rate: do not exceed **baseline + 0.10%** sustained over 10 minutes for the canary scope
- p95 latency: do not exceed **baseline + 10%** sustained over 10 minutes for the canary scope
- p99 latency: do not exceed **baseline + 15%** sustained over 10 minutes for the canary scope

Notes:
- “Baseline” must be defined as the immediate pre-change period for the same scope, with the same tags.
- If baseline is unstable (incident ongoing), do not proceed.

### Pause and abort rules
- If any required gate fails:
  1) Rollout controller must **pause** automatically (if configured) or the operator must pause manually.
  2) Operator must notify #eng-sre and #eng-platform with the change link and scope.
  3) If customer impact is likely or metrics are deteriorating, **rollback** is preferred over prolonged investigation.

## Documentation requirements (change record)
A production optimization change is not compliant unless the following are recorded (Console change record or linked ticket):
- **Owner** and **on-call contact**
- **Config version IDs**: new version, prior version (rollback target)
- **Scope**: tenant(s), model(s), region(s), percentage plan
- **Blast radius classification** and justification
- **Diff summary**: what changed (include links to diff view)
- **Expected impact**: latency/cost/throughput hypothesis
- **Validation results**: schema/semantic validation status
- **Gating plan**: which gates, thresholds, and monitoring dashboards
- **Canary results**: timestamps, key metric snapshots, and explicit “proceed” decision
- **Rollback plan**: criteria to rollback and who is authorized to execute
- **Customer comms plan** (if enterprise/SLA customers impacted)

For quantization changes, additionally include:
- Eval harness run ID(s), prompt set(s), and pass/fail criteria
- Summary of quality deltas and any known tradeoffs

## Change windows and operational constraints
- Medium/high blast radius changes must be executed during staffed hours for the primary owning team and SRE (unless declared incident/emergency).
- Avoid starting high blast radius rollouts within 60 minutes of major runtime releases unless explicitly coordinated with Release Engineering.
- Cooldown: do not apply a second high blast radius optimization change to the same global scope within 24 hours unless incident-driven.

## Emergency change procedure (Sev response)
During a Sev incident, speed and customer protection take priority.

Emergency allowances:
- SRE On-Call may execute rollback without the full approval chain if gates fail or customer impact is present.
- Documentation may be minimal at time of action, but must be completed within **24 hours**.

Emergency requirements (still mandatory):
- Use the rollback workflow (Console/API) so audit events are emitted.
- Capture incident link and request IDs in the change record.

## Private deployments (customer-managed)
For Redwood Private environments:
- Customers/operators must pin an Optimization Config Version via supported tooling (installer/helm/terraform).
- Redwood recommends following the same blast radius and canary principles where feasible.
- Rollback must follow the operator guide and should be rehearsed during onboarding for regulated workloads.

Redwood support expectations:
- Support can guide customers through rollback procedures but must not bypass customer change controls.
- Where contractual SLAs require it, ensure audit log export is configured so rollback events are retained externally.

## Enforcement and exceptions
- RBAC enforces who can apply/promote/rollback. Requests for access must go through standard access review.
- Exceptions (e.g., skipping canary steps) require written approval from SRE lead and the owning engineering lead, and must be recorded with rationale.

## Appendix A: Minimal “good” change record template
Copy/paste into the change ticket/Console notes:
- Change name:
- Author / Operator:
- Service/scope (tenant/model/region):
- Blast radius: Low/Medium/High (reason):
- New config version:
- Rollback target version:
- Diff summary:
- Canary plan (%, duration, steps):
- Gates (thresholds):
- Dashboards:
- Canary results + decision:
- Rollout completion time:
- Rollback criteria:
- Approvals:

## Appendix B: Common failure modes (use as pre-flight checklist)
- Mis-scoped apply (wrong region or tenant)
- Cache invalidation causing transient p99 spikes
- Batching delay increases impacting interactive workloads
- Quantization profile regression not caught by eval coverage gaps
- Mixed worker state due to concurrent apply/rollback (ensure rollout controller state is healthy)

## Contact / ownership
- Policy owner: Release Engineering (Connor O'Brien)
- Operational owner for rollouts/rollback: SRE (Sean Gallagher)
- Security requirements (audit/RBAC): Security & Compliance (Naomi Feldman / Marcus Lin)

Questions or proposed changes to this policy should be filed as a ticket and reviewed by the owners above.

source.title: governance-lattice-and-procedure-templates-2026
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This document defines Redwood Inference’s cross-functional governance lattice: a compact, operational catalog of company-wide policies and the workflow templates teams must use for access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement and expenses, vendor management, and travel. It is intended for process owners, managers, and auditors who run or depend on these corporate functions.

Scope

- Applies to all full-time employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to Redwood systems and data.
- Covers the following policy families: Access & Permissions, Change Management, Data Management, Procurement & Expenses, Vendor Management, Travel, and shared Templates used by teams.

Why a governance lattice?

The lattice model privileges intersecting ownership (technical owners + business owners + legal/compliance) rather than a single central gatekeeper. This reduces single points of friction while preserving auditability and consistent controls.

Executive summary of controls

- Least privilege by default: role-based groups in IAM with quarterly attestation.
- Change management: three-tier change model (low, standard, critical) with automated gating for critical changes.
- Data classification: four tiers (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) with handling procedures and retention windows.
- Procurement & spend: delegated approval matrix with automated PO creation for purchases > $5,000.
- Vendor onboarding: security, legal, and procurement must sign off before production access.
- Travel: pre-approval, expense caps, and required receipt policy for reimbursements.

Table: Policy Owners (text table)

Role | Primary Owner | Secondary Owner | Review Cadence
- Access & Permissions | Identity & Access Team | Eng Security | Quarterly
- Change Management | Eng Platform | SRE | Monthly
- Data Management | Data Governance | Legal | Annually
- Procurement & Expenses | Finance | Procurement Ops | Quarterly
- Vendor Management | Procurement | Security | On onboarding
- Travel Policy | People Ops | Finance | Annually

Access & Permissions

Principles
- Default to least privilege.
- Use group-based roles; avoid ad-hoc user-level permissions.
- All privileged roles require MFA and hardware-backed keys for production access.

Processes
1) Requesting access:
   - Submit: Use the Access Request template (see Templates section).
   - Approval chain: team lead -> service owner -> IAM admin (automated where possible).
   - Duration: temporary access expires automatically; permanent requests require justification and attestation.
2) Quarterly attestation:
   - Managers receive a review bundle showing current access for their teams; re-certify or request revocation within 7 business days.
3) Emergency access (break-glass):
   - Time-limited (max 8 hours), logged in the Emergency Access ledger, and requires post-use review by Security and the responsible manager within 48 hours.

Technical controls and SLOs
- All console logins to production services require MFA and are logged to AuditLog service (retention: 365 days).
- Provisioning latency SLO: 95% of standard role grants executed in < 4 hours.

Change Management

Change tiers
- Low (config tweaks, documentation edits) — no formal CAB; auto-approve after CI test pass.
- Standard (non-disruptive infra changes, model rollout config updates) — requires automated test results + owner approval.
- Critical (schema migrations, runtime changes affecting >5% of traffic, kernel updates) — requires formal RFC, pre-deploy canary, and CAB approval.

Procedure for Standard and Critical changes
1) Create Change Request (CR) using the Change Request template. Include rollback plan, safety checks, and traffic impact analysis.
2) Run automated preflight tests in staging.
3) Schedule canary: 5–10% traffic for at least 30 minutes with health metrics and monitoring hooks.
4) CAB review (for Critical): CAB includes Eng Platform, SRE, Security, Product owner, and a Finance representative for high-cost rollouts.
5) Post-deploy verification and 24h watch.

SLOs and thresholds
- Canary health: error rate must remain within 2x baseline; CPU/GPU usage increases must be < 30% for the canary window.
- Rollback threshold: 3x baseline error rate or sustained latency above SLO for 10 minutes.

Data Management

Classification and handling
- Public: safe to publish. No retention beyond business need.
- Internal: operational data for employees. Access: company directory groups. Retention: default 2 years.
- Confidential: customer or internal IP. Encryption at rest and transit mandatory. Retention: per contract (minimum 1 year).
- Restricted: regulated PII/PHI or customer-sensitive secrets. Access requires documented business justification and periodic attestation.

Data lifecycle controls
- Ingest: source classification, schema validation, and provenance metadata required.
- Storage: encryption keys managed in KMS; restricted-tier data must be isolated in dedicated storage namespaces.
- Retention & deletion: automated retention jobs; deletion requests must follow Data Deletion Request template with owner approval.
- Auditing: all accesses to Restricted-tier data yield detailed audit trails retained for 3 years.

Change of classification
- Any re-classification to a higher sensitivity requires a Data Impact Assessment and Legal review.

Procurement & Expenses

Approval matrix (text)
- Individual contributor: up to $500 — manager approval.
- Manager: up to $5,000 — manager + finance notification.
- Director: up to $25,000 — director + procurement approval.
- VP+: > $25,000 — procurement + finance + VP sign-off.

Process highlights
- All vendor spend must be raised via the Procurement portal to generate a PO before service activation for amounts > $5,000.
- Corporate card use: receipts required for all transactions; monthly reconciliation due 10 business days after month end.
- Capital expense vs operating expense: Finance retains final classification authority and will flag exceptions during monthly close.

Vendor Management

Onboarding checklist (required)
1) Vendor information: legal name, tax ID, contractual contact.
2) Security assessment: complete Security Questionnaire and provide SOC2/ISO evidence where applicable.
3) Legal: NDA and Master Services Agreement signed.
4) Procurement: PO issued and terms captured.
5) Access: limiting principle — vendors granted only the minimal access necessary; all vendor access must be time-bound and logged.

Ongoing reviews
- High-risk vendors: quarterly security posture review and annual business review.
- Low-risk vendors: annual contract review.

Travel Policy (summary)

Booking & approvals
- All travel must be pre-approved via the Travel Request template. Approvers: direct manager for domestic travel; director-level approval required for international travel.
- Preferred suppliers: use corporate Travel Portal to receive negotiated rates.

Expense reimbursement
- Receipts required for all meals, transportation, and lodging. Card transactions without receipts must be explained in the Expense Note and may be denied.
- Per diem: set per region; exceptions require pre-approval.

Safety & Security
- Emergency contact: People Ops + Security on call.
- Travel to high-risk regions requires Security sign-off and mandatory pre-travel briefing.

Templates & Example Artifacts

- Access Request template (example snippet):

  {
    "requester": "<email>",
    "resource": "<service-name>",
    "role_requested": "<role>",
    "justification": "<business reason>",
    "duration": "<hours/days/perm>",
    "manager_approval": "<manager-email>"
  }

- Change Request template (example snippet):

  Title: <short title>
  Owner: <service owner name/email>
  Tier: low | standard | critical
  Description: <what and why>
  Impact: <approx traffic/users/costs>
  Rollback plan: <steps>
  Approval signatures: <list>

- Data Deletion Request (example snippet):

  Requestor: <email>
  Data scope: <table of datasets>
  Reason: <business/legal>
  Desired deletion date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
  Data owner sign-off: <name>

Standard process flow (text diagram)
- Request -> Triage -> Owner review -> Automated tests -> Preflight -> Canary -> CAB (if required) -> Deploy -> Post-deploy watch -> Close

Roles & Responsibilities

- Service owner: technical owner accountable for feature behavior, deployment, and rollback plans.
- Process owner: maintains the policy text, templates, and review cadence. Usually mapped to a central function (e.g., Finance for procurement).
- Approvers: named individuals authorized to sign on approvals in the matrix.
- IAM admin: operationally executes access changes and maintains automation.

Escalation path

1) First-line: team lead and service owner (0–1 hour)
2) Second-line: process owner and security/finance as appropriate (1–4 hours)
3) Executive escalation: VP-level (4+ hours)

Implementation & Adoption Plan

- Q1: Rollout of Access attestation automation and procurement PO enforcement for > $5,000.
- Q2: Enforce Change Request gating for critical changes and integrate canary automation into CI.
- Q3: Complete Data classification tagging across datasets and begin Restricted-tier isolation.

Audit & Compliance

- Monthly compliance snapshot shared with Execs: access compliance %, outstanding attestation items, open high-risk vendor issues.
- Annual external audit for SOC2 readiness mapped to Data Management and Access controls.

Appendix: Quick links and references

- IAM console: https://console.internal.redwood-inference.com/iam
- Procurement portal: https://procure.internal.redwood-inference.com
- Change Request board (templates): /confluence/eng-platform/change-management/templates
- Data classification rubric: /confluence/policies-and-process/data-classification-rubric-2024

Revision history

- 2025-06-12: Initial publication.
- 2025-12-04: Added vendor onboarding checklist and emergency access ledger requirements.
- 2026-02-10: Updated procurement approval limits and added canary thresholds for change management.

Contact

For questions about this document, contact the Process Owner: priya.natarajan@redwood-inference.com or raise an item in #policy-questions on Slack.
