text:
Overview

This document defines Redwood Inference’s cross-functional governance lattice: a compact, operational catalog of company-wide policies and the workflow templates teams must use for access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement and expenses, vendor management, and travel. It is intended for process owners, managers, and auditors who run or depend on these corporate functions.

Scope

- Applies to all full-time employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to Redwood systems and data.
- Covers the following policy families: Access & Permissions, Change Management, Data Management, Procurement & Expenses, Vendor Management, Travel, and shared Templates used by teams.

Why a governance lattice?

The lattice model privileges intersecting ownership (technical owners + business owners + legal/compliance) rather than a single central gatekeeper. This reduces single points of friction while preserving auditability and consistent controls.

Executive summary of controls

- Least privilege by default: role-based groups in IAM with quarterly attestation.
- Change management: three-tier change model (low, standard, critical) with automated gating for critical changes.
- Data classification: four tiers (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) with handling procedures and retention windows.
- Procurement & spend: delegated approval matrix with automated PO creation for purchases > $5,000.
- Vendor onboarding: security, legal, and procurement must sign off before production access.
- Travel: pre-approval, expense caps, and required receipt policy for reimbursements.

Table: Policy Owners (text table)

Role | Primary Owner | Secondary Owner | Review Cadence
- Access & Permissions | Identity & Access Team | Eng Security | Quarterly
- Change Management | Eng Platform | SRE | Monthly
- Data Management | Data Governance | Legal | Annually
- Procurement & Expenses | Finance | Procurement Ops | Quarterly
- Vendor Management | Procurement | Security | On onboarding
- Travel Policy | People Ops | Finance | Annually

Access & Permissions

Principles
- Default to least privilege.
- Use group-based roles; avoid ad-hoc user-level permissions.
- All privileged roles require MFA and hardware-backed keys for production access.

Processes
1) Requesting access:
   - Submit: Use the Access Request template (see Templates section).
   - Approval chain: team lead -> service owner -> IAM admin (automated where possible).
   - Duration: temporary access expires automatically; permanent requests require justification and attestation.
2) Quarterly attestation:
   - Managers receive a review bundle showing current access for their teams; re-certify or request revocation within 7 business days.
3) Emergency access (break-glass):
   - Time-limited (max 8 hours), logged in the Emergency Access ledger, and requires post-use review by Security and the responsible manager within 48 hours.

Technical controls and SLOs
- All console logins to production services require MFA and are logged to AuditLog service (retention: 365 days).
- Provisioning latency SLO: 95% of standard role grants executed in < 4 hours.

Change Management

Change tiers
- Low (config tweaks, documentation edits) — no formal CAB; auto-approve after CI test pass.
- Standard (non-disruptive infra changes, model rollout config updates) — requires automated test results + owner approval.
- Critical (schema migrations, runtime changes affecting >5% of traffic, kernel updates) — requires formal RFC, pre-deploy canary, and CAB approval.

Procedure for Standard and Critical changes
1) Create Change Request (CR) using the Change Request template. Include rollback plan, safety checks, and traffic impact analysis.
2) Run automated preflight tests in staging.
3) Schedule canary: 5–10% traffic for at least 30 minutes with health metrics and monitoring hooks.
4) CAB review (for Critical): CAB includes Eng Platform, SRE, Security, Product owner, and a Finance representative for high-cost rollouts.
5) Post-deploy verification and 24h watch.

SLOs and thresholds
- Canary health: error rate must remain within 2x baseline; CPU/GPU usage increases must be < 30% for the canary window.
- Rollback threshold: 3x baseline error rate or sustained latency above SLO for 10 minutes.

Data Management

Classification and handling
- Public: safe to publish. No retention beyond business need.
- Internal: operational data for employees. Access: company directory groups. Retention: default 2 years.
- Confidential: customer or internal IP. Encryption at rest and transit mandatory. Retention: per contract (minimum 1 year).
- Restricted: regulated PII/PHI or customer-sensitive secrets. Access requires documented business justification and periodic attestation.

Data lifecycle controls
- Ingest: source classification, schema validation, and provenance metadata required.
- Storage: encryption keys managed in KMS; restricted-tier data must be isolated in dedicated storage namespaces.
- Retention & deletion: automated retention jobs; deletion requests must follow Data Deletion Request template with owner approval.
- Auditing: all accesses to Restricted-tier data yield detailed audit trails retained for 3 years.

Change of classification
- Any re-classification to a higher sensitivity requires a Data Impact Assessment and Legal review.

Procurement & Expenses

Approval matrix (text)
- Individual contributor: up to $500 — manager approval.
- Manager: up to $5,000 — manager + finance notification.
- Director: up to $25,000 — director + procurement approval.
- VP+: > $25,000 — procurement + finance + VP sign-off.

Process highlights
- All vendor spend must be raised via the Procurement portal to generate a PO before service activation for amounts > $5,000.
- Corporate card use: receipts required for all transactions; monthly reconciliation due 10 business days after month end.
- Capital expense vs operating expense: Finance retains final classification authority and will flag exceptions during monthly close.

Vendor Management

Onboarding checklist (required)
1) Vendor information: legal name, tax ID, contractual contact.
2) Security assessment: complete Security Questionnaire and provide SOC2/ISO evidence where applicable.
3) Legal: NDA and Master Services Agreement signed.
4) Procurement: PO issued and terms captured.
5) Access: limiting principle — vendors granted only the minimal access necessary; all vendor access must be time-bound and logged.

Ongoing reviews
- High-risk vendors: quarterly security posture review and annual business review.
- Low-risk vendors: annual contract review.

Travel Policy (summary)

Booking & approvals
- All travel must be pre-approved via the Travel Request template. Approvers: direct manager for domestic travel; director-level approval required for international travel.
- Preferred suppliers: use corporate Travel Portal to receive negotiated rates.

Expense reimbursement
- Receipts required for all meals, transportation, and lodging. Card transactions without receipts must be explained in the Expense Note and may be denied.
- Per diem: set per region; exceptions require pre-approval.

Safety & Security
- Emergency contact: People Ops + Security on call.
- Travel to high-risk regions requires Security sign-off and mandatory pre-travel briefing.

Templates & Example Artifacts

- Access Request template (example snippet):

  {
    "requester": "<email>",
    "resource": "<service-name>",
    "role_requested": "<role>",
    "justification": "<business reason>",
    "duration": "<hours/days/perm>",
    "manager_approval": "<manager-email>"
  }

- Change Request template (example snippet):

  Title: <short title>
  Owner: <service owner name/email>
  Tier: low | standard | critical
  Description: <what and why>
  Impact: <approx traffic/users/costs>
  Rollback plan: <steps>
  Approval signatures: <list>

- Data Deletion Request (example snippet):

  Requestor: <email>
  Data scope: <table of datasets>
  Reason: <business/legal>
  Desired deletion date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
  Data owner sign-off: <name>

Standard process flow (text diagram)
- Request -> Triage -> Owner review -> Automated tests -> Preflight -> Canary -> CAB (if required) -> Deploy -> Post-deploy watch -> Close

Roles & Responsibilities

- Service owner: technical owner accountable for feature behavior, deployment, and rollback plans.
- Process owner: maintains the policy text, templates, and review cadence. Usually mapped to a central function (e.g., Finance for procurement).
- Approvers: named individuals authorized to sign on approvals in the matrix.
- IAM admin: operationally executes access changes and maintains automation.

Escalation path

1) First-line: team lead and service owner (0–1 hour)
2) Second-line: process owner and security/finance as appropriate (1–4 hours)
3) Executive escalation: VP-level (4+ hours)

Implementation & Adoption Plan

- Q1: Rollout of Access attestation automation and procurement PO enforcement for > $5,000.
- Q2: Enforce Change Request gating for critical changes and integrate canary automation into CI.
- Q3: Complete Data classification tagging across datasets and begin Restricted-tier isolation.

Audit & Compliance

- Monthly compliance snapshot shared with Execs: access compliance %, outstanding attestation items, open high-risk vendor issues.
- Annual external audit for SOC2 readiness mapped to Data Management and Access controls.

Appendix: Quick links and references

- IAM console: https://console.internal.redwood-inference.com/iam
- Procurement portal: https://procure.internal.redwood-inference.com
- Change Request board (templates): /confluence/eng-platform/change-management/templates
- Data classification rubric: /confluence/policies-and-process/data-classification-rubric-2024

Revision history

- 2025-06-12: Initial publication.
- 2025-12-04: Added vendor onboarding checklist and emergency access ledger requirements.
- 2026-02-10: Updated procurement approval limits and added canary thresholds for change management.

Contact

For questions about this document, contact the Process Owner: priya.natarajan@redwood-inference.com or raise an item in #policy-questions on Slack.

source.title: Integrated Controls and Procedures Playbook
source.author: Maya Chen
source.summary: Company-wide single reference for access and permissions, change management, data handling, procurement and expenses, vendor lifecycle, travel rules, and common templates. Designed to standardize decision points, approval thresholds, and escalation paths across Redwood.
source.content:
Purpose

This playbook consolidates cross-functional controls and operational procedures that apply company-wide. It is intended for team leads, approvers, oncall engineers, procurement coordinators, and people-ops partners. Use this as the canonical reference for day-to-day decisions, escalations, and exception requests.

Scope

Applies to all Redwood employees, contractors, and vendors interacting with company systems or incurring expenses on Redwood behalf. Sections cover: access and permissions, change management, data classification and lifecycle, procurement and expenses, vendor management, travel policy, and reusable internal templates.

Owners and Contact Matrix

- Document owner: finance-legal (primary)
- Operational steward per area:
  - Access & Permissions: Security & Engineering IAM team (security@redwood.example.com)
  - Change Management: Eng Platform (platform-oncall@redwood.example.com)
  - Data Management: Data Governance (data-governance@redwood.example.com)
  - Procurement & Expenses: Finance Operations (procure@redwood.example.com)
  - Vendor Management: Vendor Ops (vendor-ops@redwood.example.com)
  - Travel & Reimbursements: People Ops (people-ops@redwood.example.com)

1) Access and Permissions

Objective: least privilege, auditable changes, fast onboarding/offboarding.

Key policies:
- Role-based access is the default. Every resource must map to one or more approved roles from the Role Definition Index.
- Approval chain: manager -> resource owner -> IAM admin for elevated scopes.
- Just-in-time (JIT) access: temporary elevated access requests are limited to 8 hours by default and must include a justification and expiry.
- Offboarding: account disable within 2 hours of termination notice; artifacts and keys rotated within 24 hours.

Standards and thresholds:
- Production cluster write-level access: requires engineering manager + security lead approval. Emergency break-glass follows the incident runbook and must be documented within 24 hours.
- Secrets access: use KMS/HSM integrations only; direct key material export is prohibited.

Request flow (example):
1. Submit request via IAM portal (link in Related Pages).
2. Manager approves within 48 business hours.
3. Resource owner confirms within 48 business hours.
4. IAM admin executes and timestamps the change; audit event auto-created.

2) Change Management

Goal: minimize user impact while enabling continuous delivery.

Change categories:
- Standard change: pre-approved, low-risk, repeatable (eg configuration updates that pass automated tests). No committee approval required.
- Normal change: requires risk assessment and approvals per the risk matrix below.
- Emergency change: applied to mitigate active incidents; must be post-fact reviewed within 72 hours.

Risk matrix (summary):
| Impact level | Examples | Approval required |
|-------------|----------|------------------|
| Low | Non-prod config, cosmetic UI | Line manager |
| Medium | Production config, non-customer facing | Team lead + Platform owner |
| High | Schema changes, infra changes affecting latency | Eng director + Security + SRE on-call |

Process steps for Normal change:
1. Create change ticket with description, roll back plan, test plan, and blast radius.
2. Automated checks: CI lint, integration smoke tests, perf budget check.
3. Approvals collected via change management tool; SLA for approvers is 72 business hours.
4. Schedule in a maintenance window or follow canary release policy.
5. Post-deploy verification and telemetry review; close ticket with post-mortem if deviation occurs.

Emergency change rules:
- Any emergency change must be annotated with incident id and emergency approver name.
- A retro with timeline and rationale is required within 72 hours and will be retained for 12 months.

3) Data Management

Classification scheme:
- Public: safe for external consumption.
- Internal: company-internal operational data.
- Restricted: contains sensitive customer or regulated data. Access requires explicit approval and encryption at rest and in transit.

Retention and deletion:
- Default retention for internal logs: 180 days.
- Audit logs: 2 years.
- Customer data: retention per contract; default 1 year unless specified.

Handling rules:
- Restricted data must never be stored in public cloud object stores without encryption and a customer-specific ACL.
- PII and documents with restricted flags require a data processing addendum and must be routed through the data governance intake.

Data export and sharing:
- Exports of restricted data require a data export request including purpose, destination, and approval signatures from data-governance and legal.

4) Procurement and Expenses

Principles: cost effectiveness, vendor consolidation where appropriate, and clear approvals to manage risk.

Approval thresholds (examples):
- Up to $2,000: direct manager approval.
- $2,000 to $25,000: finance operations approval + procurement coordinator.
- Over $25,000: VP-level approval and legal review.

Procurement lifecycle:
1. Initiation: submit purchase request with business justification and vendor details.
2. Vendor validation: security questionnaire and SOW review if applicable.
3. Approval: obtain signatures per thresholds. 4. POs issued by Finance.
5. Receipt and invoice matching, payment per net terms.

Expense policy highlights:
- Use company corporate card for approved travel and vendor charges when available.
- Receipts required for all expenses over $25. Reimbursement windows: submit within 60 days.

5) Vendor Management

Vendor tiers:
- Tier 1: strategic vendors with contractual SLAs and at-scale access to sensitive data.
- Tier 2: operational vendors with non-sensitive access.
- Tier 3: one-off consultants and contractors.

Onboarding checklist:
- Security questionnaire completion (NDA if required)
- Data handling and PCI/PII assessment if applicable
- Contract with clear termination, liability, and SLOs
- Assigned vendor owner and contact info in vendor registry

Ongoing oversight:
- Quarterly health reviews for Tier 1 vendors; annual review for Tier 2.
- Continuous monitoring for security signals; automated alerts on unusual access patterns.

Offboarding:
- Revoke all credentials and remove network access within 48 hours of contract termination.
- Verify data purge per contract and log the verification.

6) Travel and Reimbursements

Travel approval flows:
- Domestic travel under $1,000: manager pre-approval.
- International travel or any trip requiring visa: prior approval from People Ops and manager.

Booking and preferred vendors:
- Use the corporate travel portal where feasible. Preferred hotels and carriers negotiated by Finance will be listed in the Travel Guide.

Per diem and meal limits:
- Domestic per diem: up to $75/day for meals.
- International per diem: country-specific; check Travel Guide.

Safety and exceptions:
- If travel is to a location with an active travel advisory, approval from the Head of People Ops is required.

7) Templates and Artifacts

Included templates (copyable from this page):
- Change Request Template (fields: title, owner, blast radius, rollback plan, test plan, schedule)
- Procurement Request Template (vendor, estimated cost, business justification, required approvals)
- Vendor Onboarding Checklist
- Data Export Request Form

Example Change Request Template (plain text):
- Title: 
- Owner: 
- Impact area: 
- Rollback plan: 
- Test plan: 
- Approvals required: 
- Schedule window: 

8) Exceptions, Escalations, and Amendments

- Exceptions may be requested via the Exception Request form and must include a mitigation plan and sunset date.
- Escalation path: approver -> approver's manager -> document owner -> Head of Finance-Legal (for procurement) or Head of Security (for access/data).
- Policy amendments: any section owner may propose edits; changes to approval thresholds require sign-off from Finance-Legal and People Ops.

Appendix: Example thresholds and SLOs

- IAM request approval SLA: 48 business hours for standard requests.
- Change approver SLA: 72 business hours.
- Emergency post-fact review required within 72 hours.
- Vendor credential removal after termination: 48 hours.

Related links and artifacts

- Role Definition Index: see Related Pages
- Procurement portal and PO tracker: finance internal tools
- IAM portal: link on internal wiki

Revision history

- 2024-11-05: initial draft published by Maya Chen
- 2025-06-12: updated procurement thresholds and added vendor tiers
- 2026-02-10: alignment with new IAM JIT tooling and clarified emergency change retro SLA

Notes

This playbook is intentionally prescriptive on approval thresholds and timelines to reduce ambiguity. Teams may request exceptions but expect compensating controls. For operational detail, follow linked runbooks and the Role Definition Index.

- Assigned vendor owner and contact info in vendor registry

Ongoing oversight:
- Quarterly health reviews for Tier 1 vendors; annual review for Tier 2.
- Continuous monitoring for security signals; automated alerts on unusual access patterns.

Offboarding:
- Revoke all credentials and remove network access within 48 hours of contract termination.
- Verify data purge per contract and log the verification.

6) Travel and Reimbursements

Travel approval flows:
- Domestic travel under $1,000: manager pre-approval.
- International travel or any trip requiring visa: prior approval from People Ops and manager.

Booking and preferred vendors:
- Use the corporate travel portal where feasible. Preferred hotels and carriers negotiated by Finance will be listed in the Travel Guide.

Per diem and meal limits:
- Domestic per diem: up to $75/day for meals.
- International per diem: country-specific; check Travel Guide.

Safety and exceptions:
- If travel is to a location with an active travel advisory, approval from the Head of People Ops is required.

7) Templates and Artifacts

Included templates (copyable from this page):
- Change Request Template (fields: title, owner, blast radius, rollback plan, test plan, schedule)
- Procurement Request Template (vendor, estimated cost, business justification, required approvals)
- Vendor Onboarding Checklist
- Data Export Request Form

Example Change Request Template (plain text):
- Title: 
- Owner: 
- Impact area: 
- Rollback plan: 
- Test plan: 
- Approvals required: 
- Schedule window: 

8) Exceptions, Escalations, and Amendments

- Exceptions may be requested via the Exception Request form and must include a mitigation plan and sunset date.
- Escalation path: approver -> approver's manager -> document owner -> Head of Finance-Legal (for procurement) or Head of Security (for access/data).
- Policy amendments: any section owner may propose edits; changes to approval thresholds require sign-off from Finance-Legal and People Ops.

Appendix: Example thresholds and SLOs

- IAM request approval SLA: 48 business hours for standard requests.
- Change approver SLA: 72 business hours.
- Emergency post-fact review required within 72 hours.
- Vendor credential removal after termination: 48 hours.

Related links and artifacts

- Role Definition Index: see Related Pages
- Procurement portal and PO tracker: finance internal tools
- IAM portal: link on internal wiki

Revision history

- 2024-11-05: initial draft published by Maya Chen
- 2025-06-12: updated procurement thresholds and added vendor tiers
- 2026-02-10: alignment with new IAM JIT tooling and clarified emergency change retro SLA

Notes

This playbook is intentionally prescriptive on approval thresholds and timelines to reduce ambiguity. Teams may request exceptions but expect compensating controls. For operational detail, follow linked runbooks and the Role Definition Index.

source.title: Lifecycle Email + Retargeting Data Handling Policy (Hosted API)
source.author: Olga Petrov
source.content:
## Summary
This policy defines what user events and attributes may be used for (1) lifecycle onboarding emails and (2) paid retargeting campaigns supporting Redwood Inference’s Hosted API self-serve motion. It also defines retention limits, audience exclusion rules (including enterprise suppression), and the compliance checklist required prior to launching or scaling a campaign.

This policy exists to ensure:
- Data minimization (use the least data needed)
- Clear lawful basis for processing and marketing outreach
- Consistent opt-out handling across all lifecycle surfaces
- Protection of sensitive customers (regulated accounts, active enterprise opportunities)
- Consistent retention and deletion expectations

## Scope
In scope:
- Lifecycle onboarding emails and product education emails triggered by signup and early product milestones
- In-console email preferences/unsubscribe management
- Retargeting via ad platforms (e.g., LinkedIn/Google/Meta) using website pixels and/or hashed identifiers
- Audience building using Redwood first-party event data (activation funnel events)

Out of scope:
- Sales outreach sequences (handled by Sales Ops policies)
- Customer support notifications (transactional communications)
- Product analytics for non-marketing purposes (see Analytics Data Policy)

## Definitions
- **Lifecycle email (marketing):** Non-transactional emails intended to drive activation/retention (e.g., “send your first request”).
- **Transactional email:** Required service communications (e.g., password reset, security alerts). Transactional emails are not governed by marketing opt-out, but must follow standard deliverability and legal requirements.
- **Retargeting:** Paid ads shown to users based on prior site/app behavior (pixel-based or list-based).
- **Activation funnel events:** Standard events representing signup → API key created → first successful request → first 100k tokens.
- **Active enterprise opportunity:** An account/domain with an open Sales opportunity in HubSpot or otherwise flagged by Sales leadership for suppression.

## Systems and data flow (reference)
Primary systems involved:
- **Product/Console eventing** (first-party events)
- **Lifecycle email provider** (send + unsub + preference center)
- **Ad platforms** (audiences + conversions)
- **HubSpot** (deal stage, account flags, suppression lists)
- **Data warehouse** (aggregation and reporting; access-controlled)

Approved high-level flow:
1) Product emits minimal activation events.
2) Warehouse aggregates eligibility cohorts (no raw prompt content).
3) Lifecycle provider receives only the fields required for triggers and suppression.
4) Retargeting audiences are built either from consented pixel events or from hashed identifiers with strict exclusions.

## Data minimization principles (non-negotiable)
For lifecycle email and retargeting, **do not use**:
- Prompt text, completion text, embeddings, message history, tool calls, or any customer-provided content
- Raw error payloads that may contain customer content
- Full IP address, device fingerprinting beyond what is needed for consented measurement
- Any “special category” data (GDPR) or inferred sensitive attributes

Allowed identifiers (when needed):
- Internal user_id (pseudonymous)
- Internal account_id / workspace_id
- Email address (only within lifecycle email provider; do not store in ad platforms except as hashed audience uploads, when approved)
- Domain (for suppression and aggregation; avoid exporting domains to ad platforms)

## Approved event categories and usage
The following table describes which event types are approved for lifecycle email and/or retargeting.

| Category | Examples (illustrative) | Allowed use | Restrictions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signup + account creation | signup_created, workspace_created | Email triggers; retargeting segmentation | Must not include freeform text fields. |
| API key + auth setup | api_key_created, api_key_failed | Email triggers; console nudges measurement | Do not include key material; only boolean success/failure. |
| First request / activation | first_request_succeeded, first_request_failed, ttft_ms | Email triggers (e.g., stalled), measurement | Failure events must use error codes only; no request payloads. |
| Usage milestones | tokens_10k_reached, tokens_100k_reached, active_day | Email timing (education), measurement | Use aggregated counters; no per-request logs in marketing tools. |
| Docs/quickstart engagement | docs_quickstart_clicked, hello_world_copied | Measurement and segmentation | Use URL path/category only; avoid query strings if they can contain PII. |
| Email interactions | email_delivered, email_opened, email_clicked, unsubscribed | Measurement, suppression | Opens may be unreliable (privacy); never gate compliance actions on opens. |
| Consent + preferences | consent_marketing_opt_in/out, preferences_updated | Suppression, compliance evidence | Must be system-of-record and replicated to all send systems. |

## Required event properties (approved schema)
When activation events are used for marketing triggers or audience definitions, properties must be limited to:
- timestamp (UTC)
- user_id, account_id
- region (coarse: us/eu/apac)
- plan_tier (free/trial/paid)
- sdk_language (optional, e.g., typescript/python)
- activation_stage (derived, not raw request data)
- error_code (if applicable; no stack traces)

Explicitly prohibited event properties for marketing tooling:
- prompt, completion, messages, tool_call_args
- raw URL query parameters
- ip_address (full)
- any field marked customer_content=true

## Lawful basis and consent requirements
### Lifecycle email (marketing)
- Default lawful basis: **Legitimate Interest** for onboarding/education for new signups (where permitted).
- Where required (jurisdiction/vendor policy), use **Consent** for marketing email beyond strictly onboarding.
- Regardless of lawful basis, Redwood must provide:
  - Clear identification of sender
  - A working unsubscribe mechanism
  - Preference management (where applicable)
  - Timely suppression after opt-out

### Retargeting
- Pixel-based retargeting must respect cookie/ads consent requirements by region.
- For EU/EEA/UK traffic:
  - If consent is not obtained for advertising cookies, the user must not be added to retargeting audiences.
  - Consent state must be auditable and queryable for suppression.

## Audience definitions: approved rules and exclusions
### General rules (all audiences)
- Build audiences using **minimal eligibility logic** and **short lookback windows**.
- Apply exclusions first (opt-outs, sensitive accounts, enterprise suppression).
- Avoid combining signals in ways that could infer sensitive categories (e.g., healthcare + churn risk).

### Mandatory exclusions (email + retargeting)
Exclude any user/account that matches any of the following:
1) **Unsubscribed / marketing opt-out** (global)
2) **Hard bounce / complaint** (suppress immediately; see Deliverability SOP)
3) **Regulated/sensitive account flags** (e.g., fintech, healthcare) when flagged “restricted tracking”
4) **Customer-managed or private deployment leads** (to avoid misaligned messaging)
5) **Active enterprise opportunity suppression** (details below)

### Enterprise opportunity suppression (retargeting guardrail)
Do not retarget users associated with:
- HubSpot deal stage: Discovery or later (or any “Open Opportunity”)
- Accounts flagged by Sales leadership as “Do Not Target”
- Domains on the weekly **Enterprise Suppression List**

Operational requirements:
- Suppression list is updated **weekly** by Sales Ops (Isabelle Laurent) and applied before any audience upload.
- For pixel-based audiences where domain suppression is not feasible, apply suppression via:
  - Excluding known hashed emails (where approved)
  - Excluding CRM-matched audiences
  - Restricting campaigns to non-enterprise geos/segments as a fallback

### Approved retargeting audience types
1) **Site visitors (consented)**
- Lookback: 14–30 days
- Landing pages: Hosted API quickstart, pricing, docs quickstart
- Exclusions: all mandatory exclusions; exclude “active users” (see below)

2) **Signup-no-activation**
- Definition: signup_created AND NOT first_request_succeeded within 7 days
- Lookback: 30 days
- Exclusions: mandatory exclusions; exclude accounts with >0 paid usage signals

3) **Activated-but-no-return**
- Definition: first_request_succeeded AND NOT active_day within 7 days after activation
- Lookback: 30 days
- Exclusions: mandatory exclusions; exclude enterprise suppression

### “Active user” suppression (to avoid wasted spend and bad UX)
For retargeting campaigns, treat a user/account as active and exclude if any of the below are true within the last 7 days:
- active_day = true
- tokens_generated > 25k (aggregated)
- ≥ 3 successful requests on Hosted API

## Retention limits and deletion
Retention and lookback windows must follow the table below unless a stricter customer agreement requires shorter retention.

| Data type | Used for | Default retention | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Marketing eligibility cohorts (derived) | Email triggers, audience building | 90 days | Store as derived flags; do not store raw content. |
| Pixel event audiences (consented) | Retargeting | 30 days | Match to consent state; must support deletion upon request. |
| Email send + interaction logs | Deliverability + measurement | 13 months | Keep minimal fields; respect vendor limits; used for seasonality comparisons. |
| Opt-out / suppression state | Compliance | Indefinite (or as required) | Must be retained to enforce opt-out and prove compliance. |
| Enterprise suppression lists | Guardrail | 12 months | Contains business metadata; access restricted to GTM ops. |

Deletion requirements:
- DSAR “Delete” requests must remove the user from:
  - lifecycle email provider lists
  - retargeting audiences (where feasible via platform tools)
  - derived cohort tables keyed by user identifiers
- Opt-out state may be retained as a suppression artifact (pseudonymous) to prevent re-adding the user.

## Vendor and sharing restrictions
- Email addresses may be shared with the lifecycle email provider as a processor under our DPA.
- For ad platforms:
  - Do not upload raw emails unless approved by Legal/Privacy and implemented as **hashed identifiers**.
  - Do not export internal user_id/account_id to ad platforms.
  - Do not export domains or company names for targeting.

## Access controls and auditability
- Access to marketing cohort tables is restricted to:
  - Growth/Marketing Ops (need-to-know)
  - Analytics owners supporting measurement
  - Legal/Privacy for audit
- All audience exports/uploads must be logged with:
  - requestor
  - date/time
  - audience definition (query/version)
  - applied exclusions
  - destination platform/account

## Compliance checklist (must be completed before launch)
Owner: Experiment owner (Ben Carter) with Marketing Ops (Tyler Benson). Privacy approver: Michael Grant or Olga Petrov.

Pre-launch:
1) Confirm audience definition is documented (human-readable + query link).
2) Confirm data fields used follow “Approved schema” (no content fields).
3) Confirm suppression logic is applied:
   - unsubscribed/opt-out
   - complaints/hard bounces
   - regulated/restricted tracking
   - enterprise opportunity suppression
   - active user suppression (retargeting)
4) Confirm cookie/ads consent gating is enforced for EU/EEA/UK retargeting.
5) Confirm unsubscribe link and sender identity requirements:
   - Unsubscribe is one-click or equivalent
   - Physical mailing address and required disclosures present
6) Confirm retention settings:
   - audiences expire within policy window
   - email logs retention configured
7) Confirm UTM conventions and attribution tags do not encode PII.
8) Confirm escalation path:
   - complaints/spam rate spike threshold (see Deliverability SOP)
   - privacy incident channel + on-call contact

Post-launch (within 7 days):
1) Validate suppression counts and sample-check for false inclusions (enterprise domains, restricted accounts).
2) Validate opt-out propagation end-to-end (provider → warehouse → triggers).
3) Validate reporting does not join to sensitive datasets or expose PII in dashboards.

## Enforcement and exceptions
- Non-compliance may result in immediate pause of sends/campaigns.
- Exceptions require written approval from Finance/Legal (Michael Grant) and must include:
  - justification
  - scope and duration
  - mitigation plan
  - rollback plan

## Contacts
- Policy owner (GDPR/compliance): Olga Petrov
- Legal (CAN-SPAM/privacy review): Michael Grant
- Marketing Ops & attribution: Tyler Benson
- Sales suppression list owner: Isabelle Laurent
- Audience exclusions & handoff rules: Jordan Blake
- Analytics/telemetry questions: Logan Wright

| Signup + account creation | signup_created, workspace_created | Email triggers; retargeting segmentation | Must not include freeform text fields. |
| API key + auth setup | api_key_created, api_key_failed | Email triggers; console nudges measurement | Do not include key material; only boolean success/failure. |
| First request / activation | first_request_succeeded, first_request_failed, ttft_ms | Email triggers (e.g., stalled), measurement | Failure events must use error codes only; no request payloads. |
| Usage milestones | tokens_10k_reached, tokens_100k_reached, active_day | Email timing (education), measurement | Use aggregated counters; no per-request logs in marketing tools. |
| Docs/quickstart engagement | docs_quickstart_clicked, hello_world_copied | Measurement and segmentation | Use URL path/category only; avoid query strings if they can contain PII. |
| Email interactions | email_delivered, email_opened, email_clicked, unsubscribed | Measurement, suppression | Opens may be unreliable (privacy); never gate compliance actions on opens. |
| Consent + preferences | consent_marketing_opt_in/out, preferences_updated | Suppression, compliance evidence | Must be system-of-record and replicated to all send systems. |

## Required event properties (approved schema)
When activation events are used for marketing triggers or audience definitions, properties must be limited to:
- timestamp (UTC)
- user_id, account_id
- region (coarse: us/eu/apac)
- plan_tier (free/trial/paid)
- sdk_language (optional, e.g., typescript/python)
- activation_stage (derived, not raw request data)
- error_code (if applicable; no stack traces)

Explicitly prohibited event properties for marketing tooling:
- prompt, completion, messages, tool_call_args
- raw URL query parameters
- ip_address (full)
- any field marked customer_content=true

## Lawful basis and consent requirements
### Lifecycle email (marketing)
- Default lawful basis: **Legitimate Interest** for onboarding/education for new signups (where permitted).
- Where required (jurisdiction/vendor policy), use **Consent** for marketing email beyond strictly onboarding.
- Regardless of lawful basis, Redwood must provide:
  - Clear identification of sender
  - A working unsubscribe mechanism
  - Preference management (where applicable)
  - Timely suppression after opt-out

### Retargeting
- Pixel-based retargeting must respect cookie/ads consent requirements by region.
- For EU/EEA/UK traffic:
  - If consent is not obtained for advertising cookies, the user must not be added to retargeting audiences.
  - Consent state must be auditable and queryable for suppression.

## Audience definitions: approved rules and exclusions
### General rules (all audiences)
- Build audiences using **minimal eligibility logic** and **short lookback windows**.
- Apply exclusions first (opt-outs, sensitive accounts, enterprise suppression).
- Avoid combining signals in ways that could infer sensitive categories (e.g., healthcare + churn risk).

### Mandatory exclusions (email + retargeting)
Exclude any user/account that matches any of the following:
1) **Unsubscribed / marketing opt-out** (global)
2) **Hard bounce / complaint** (suppress immediately; see Deliverability SOP)
3) **Regulated/sensitive account flags** (e.g., fintech, healthcare) when flagged “restricted tracking”
4) **Customer-managed or private deployment leads** (to avoid misaligned messaging)
5) **Active enterprise opportunity suppression** (details below)

### Enterprise opportunity suppression (retargeting guardrail)
Do not retarget users associated with:
- HubSpot deal stage: Discovery or later (or any “Open Opportunity”)
- Accounts flagged by Sales leadership as “Do Not Target”
- Domains on the weekly **Enterprise Suppression List**

Operational requirements:
- Suppression list is updated **weekly** by Sales Ops (Isabelle Laurent) and applied before any audience upload.
- For pixel-based audiences where domain suppression is not feasible, apply suppression via:
  - Excluding known hashed emails (where approved)
  - Excluding CRM-matched audiences
  - Restricting campaigns to non-enterprise geos/segments as a fallback

### Approved retargeting audience types
1) **Site visitors (consented)**
- Lookback: 14–30 days
- Landing pages: Hosted API quickstart, pricing, docs quickstart
- Exclusions: all mandatory exclusions; exclude “active users” (see below)

2) **Signup-no-activation**
- Definition: signup_created AND NOT first_request_succeeded within 7 days
- Lookback: 30 days
- Exclusions: mandatory exclusions; exclude accounts with >0 paid usage signals

3) **Activated-but-no-return**
- Definition: first_request_succeeded AND NOT active_day within 7 days after activation
- Lookback: 30 days
- Exclusions: mandatory exclusions; exclude enterprise suppression

### “Active user” suppression (to avoid wasted spend and bad UX)
For retargeting campaigns, treat a user/account as active and exclude if any of the below are true within the last 7 days:
- active_day = true
- tokens_generated > 25k (aggregated)
- ≥ 3 successful requests on Hosted API

## Retention limits and deletion
Retention and lookback windows must follow the table below unless a stricter customer agreement requires shorter retention.

| Data type | Used for | Default retention | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Marketing eligibility cohorts (derived) | Email triggers, audience building | 90 days | Store as derived flags; do not store raw content. |
| Pixel event audiences (consented) | Retargeting | 30 days | Match to consent state; must support deletion upon request. |
| Email send + interaction logs | Deliverability + measurement | 13 months | Keep minimal fields; respect vendor limits; used for seasonality comparisons. |
| Opt-out / suppression state | Compliance | Indefinite (or as required) | Must be retained to enforce opt-out and prove compliance. |
| Enterprise suppression lists | Guardrail | 12 months | Contains business metadata; access restricted to GTM ops. |

Deletion requirements:
- DSAR “Delete” requests must remove the user from:
  - lifecycle email provider lists
  - retargeting audiences (where feasible via platform tools)
  - derived cohort tables keyed by user identifiers
- Opt-out state may be retained as a suppression artifact (pseudonymous) to prevent re-adding the user.

## Vendor and sharing restrictions
- Email addresses may be shared with the lifecycle email provider as a processor under our DPA.
- For ad platforms:
  - Do not upload raw emails unless approved by Legal/Privacy and implemented as **hashed identifiers**.
  - Do not export internal user_id/account_id to ad platforms.
  - Do not export domains or company names for targeting.

## Access controls and auditability
- Access to marketing cohort tables is restricted to:
  - Growth/Marketing Ops (need-to-know)
  - Analytics owners supporting measurement
  - Legal/Privacy for audit
- All audience exports/uploads must be logged with:
  - requestor
  - date/time
  - audience definition (query/version)
  - applied exclusions
  - destination platform/account

## Compliance checklist (must be completed before launch)
Owner: Experiment owner (Ben Carter) with Marketing Ops (Tyler Benson). Privacy approver: Michael Grant or Olga Petrov.

Pre-launch:
1) Confirm audience definition is documented (human-readable + query link).
2) Confirm data fields used follow “Approved schema” (no content fields).
3) Confirm suppression logic is applied:
   - unsubscribed/opt-out
   - complaints/hard bounces
   - regulated/restricted tracking
   - enterprise opportunity suppression
   - active user suppression (retargeting)
4) Confirm cookie/ads consent gating is enforced for EU/EEA/UK retargeting.
5) Confirm unsubscribe link and sender identity requirements:
   - Unsubscribe is one-click or equivalent
   - Physical mailing address and required disclosures present
6) Confirm retention settings:
   - audiences expire within policy window
   - email logs retention configured
7) Confirm UTM conventions and attribution tags do not encode PII.
8) Confirm escalation path:
   - complaints/spam rate spike threshold (see Deliverability SOP)
   - privacy incident channel + on-call contact

Post-launch (within 7 days):
1) Validate suppression counts and sample-check for false inclusions (enterprise domains, restricted accounts).
2) Validate opt-out propagation end-to-end (provider → warehouse → triggers).
3) Validate reporting does not join to sensitive datasets or expose PII in dashboards.

## Enforcement and exceptions
- Non-compliance may result in immediate pause of sends/campaigns.
- Exceptions require written approval from Finance/Legal (Michael Grant) and must include:
  - justification
  - scope and duration
  - mitigation plan
  - rollback plan

## Contacts
- Policy owner (GDPR/compliance): Olga Petrov
- Legal (CAN-SPAM/privacy review): Michael Grant
- Marketing Ops & attribution: Tyler Benson
- Sales suppression list owner: Isabelle Laurent
- Audience exclusions & handoff rules: Jordan Blake
- Analytics/telemetry questions: Logan Wright

source.title: Operational Commons and Process Patterns 2026
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This document consolidates company-wide process patterns and canonical templates that operational teams (finance, security, people-ops, engineering, and product) should follow when executing cross-functional activities: access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the internal templates used to standardize execution.

Audience: All Redwood employees who own or execute operational processes, and technical leads who implement controls in services and tools.

Scope: Applies to all corporate functions and product teams operating under Redwood Inference legal entities. This is the canonical set of process patterns; local variations require documented exceptions and a risk-owner signoff.

-----

1) Access & Permissions

Goal: Grant least privilege access quickly while maintaining auditability and fast recovery for incidents.

Key Roles and Owners:
- Access Owner: Team manager (regionally delegated)
- Access Audit Owner: Security Team
- Provisioning Tool Owner: Eng-Platform

Pattern: Request -> Approve -> Provision -> Review

Procedure (standard):
1. Submit access request via the Access Request form (Confluence template: "access-request-template") with justification and least-privilege role.
2. Manager-level approval required for all role grants; for admin-level roles, security review is mandatory.
3. Eng-Platform or HR (for people systems) performs provisioning within 48 business hours.
4. Access grants automatically expire after 90 days unless renewed.
5. Quarterly review: Security runs an automated orphan-account and role-scope report; team managers must attest within 14 days.

SLA and Controls:
- Provisioning SLA: 48 business hours for standard requests, 5 business days for sensitive roles.
- Periodic Audit: Quarterly attestation, monthly automated scans.

Revocation and Emergency Access:
- Emergency access (break glass) requires written post-facto justification within 24 hours and is logged to the central audit log.

-----

2) Change Management (services, infra, and major configs)

Goal: Minimize unexpected impact from changes while enabling rapid iteration on serving stacks and business systems.

Change categories:
- Fast-track: Non-customer-facing, low-risk changes (doc updates, non-prod infra tuning).
- Standard: Routine production changes with rollbacks and monitoring in place.
- Major: Schema migrations, model rollouts, capacity-related infra changes, changes affecting SLAs.

Approval Matrix:
- Fast-track: Owner approval + automated CI checks.
- Standard: Owner + peer reviewer + responsible SRE on-call signoff. Rollout plan and rollback playbook required.
- Major: Owner + Eng-Platform + Product + Change Advisory Board (CAB) signoff. CAB meets twice weekly; emergency CAB can be convened.

Deployment checklist (for Standard and Major):
1. Describe change, impact, and rollback plan in the change request template.
2. Pre-deploy canary in a controlled slice (1–5% traffic or designated test cluster).
3. Instrumentation: Ensure tracing, profiling, and key metric dashboards are updated.
4. Run smoke tests and the regression prompt-set where model-serving behavior might change.
5. Perform progressive rollout using feature flags and monitor error budget and latency SLOs for 30 minutes after each step.
6. If any threshold breach occurs, trigger automatic rollback and page the on-call.

Escalation thresholds (example):
- Latency increase > 20% from baseline for 5 minutes -> immediate rollback.
- Error rate > 1% for 3 minutes -> rollback + incident review.

Change windows: Standard business hours preferred; Major changes require notified maintenance windows and customer-facing communications.

-----

3) Data Management (classification, retention, handling)

Principles: Minimize retained PII, apply defense-in-depth, and document lineage for datasets used for training/eval.

Classification tiers:
- Public: Documentation, marketing assets.
- Internal: Product specs, runbooks, non-PII operational data.
- Restricted: Customer data, secrets, regulated datasets.

Retention and Access Rules (summary table in plain text):
| Data Type | Retention Default | Access Pattern | Owner |
| Customer logs (non-PII) | 180 days | Read for support & SRE | Customer Success |
| Customer PII | 90 days (default) | Minimal, audited | Security |
| Model training datasets | 365 days (review) | Restricted; request-based | Applied ML Team |
| Audit logs | 2 years | Security & Compliance | Security |

Data Handling Requirements:
- All Restricted data must be stored in approved repositories (VPC-only buckets, customer-dedicated storage) and encrypted at rest with KMS keys.
- Masking/pseudonymization is required before using customer telemetry for model training unless explicit consent/contractual terms allow.
- Data deletion requests must be tracked via the Data Deletion Request template and completed within contractual timelines.

Lineage and Provenance:
- Every dataset used for model onboarding must include a provenance document: source, transformation steps, retention policy, and contact owner.

-----

4) Procurement & Expenses

Objective: Streamline purchases and expense reimbursement while providing delegation controls and budget visibility.

Purchase thresholds and authority matrix:
- <= $5,000: Team manager approval.
- $5,001–$50,000: Department head approval + procurement review.
- > $50,000: VP-level approval + legal/compliance review.

Approved procurement flow:
1. Create a Purchase Request (PR) using the Purchase Request template with business justification, budget code, and vendor info.
2. Procurement verifies vendor due diligence checklist (insurance, security posture questionnaire if needed).
3. Procurement creates PO and routes to vendor.
4. Finance records the commit against the budget and tracks payments.

Expense policy highlights:
- Reimbursable items: travel, client entertainment (with receipts and business justification), approved training.
- Non-reimbursable: personal expenses, fines, or items without documentation.
- Expense submission: within 30 days of expenditure; late submissions require manager exception.

Travel approvals:
- Any trip > $2,500 requires explicit manager and travel coordinator approval and a travel safety review for international travel.

-----

5) Vendor Management

Vendor lifecycle stages: Selection -> Contracting -> Onboarding -> Monitoring -> Offboarding

Minimum due diligence checklist (selection stage):
- Legal & security review (standard clause checklist).
- SOC2 or equivalent evidence for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Financial health check for long-term commitments.

Onboarding artifacts required:
- Signed contract with SLAs and termination rights.
- Security questionnaire and remediation plan.
- Contact and escalation list (vendor-side and Redwood-side).

Monitoring cadence:
- Strategic vendors: quarterly business reviews (QBRs).
- Operational vendors: monthly KPI review and incident syncs as needed.

Offboarding checklist:
- Data return or certified deletion; revoke credentials and ensure accounts terminated.
- Final invoice reconciliation and retention of contract for 7 years (per legal retention schedule).

-----

6) Travel & Event Policy (high-level)

Principles: Safety first, cost-consciousness second, equitable access to travel for required business needs.

Booking rules:
- Use approved travel portal; economy class for flights under 5 hours, premium economy or business only with pre-approved manager exception.
- Hotel selection must use corporate rates when available.

Safety & Approvals:
- International travel requires manager approval and travel safety check for high-risk countries.
- Emergency travel assistance details and insurance coverage are available through People Ops.

Expenses and per diems:
- Meal per diem caps differ by region; itemize expenses and provide receipts for amounts over $75 USD equivalent.

Conference sponsorships and exhibitor booths:
- Additional procurement flow required for significant spend on booths or sponsorships. See Procurement section.

-----

7) Internal Templates and Checklists (where to find them)

Canonical templates (use and link names):
- access-request-template (Access & Permissions)
- change-request-template (Change Management)
- procurement-purchase-request (Procurement & Expenses)
- vendor-onboarding-pack (Vendor Management)
- data-provenance-checklist (Data Management)
- travel-approval-form (Travel)
- post-incident-review (Incident Response)

Template usage rules:
- Teams must not create local forks of templates without documenting the differences and obtaining an owner-team signoff.
- All templates have a metadata header that includes template owner, last revision date, and related policy link.

-----

Governance and Exceptions

Exception process:
1. Document the exception request using the Exception Request template, including risk assessment and mitigation.
2. Route to owner_team and governance board (depending on risk tier).
3. Approval requires a documented compensating control and an expiration date.

Periodic review cycle:
- Policy and templates review cadence is annual. High-risk controls (access, data handling) are reviewed semi-annually.

Metrics and KPIs (suggested):
- Time-to-provision (median hours) for access requests.
- Change success rate (percentage of changes with no rollback).
- Vendor security compliance coverage (%) for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Expense processing time (median days).

Appendix: Example change request checklist (text)
1. Change summary and owner contact.
2. Impact analysis and affected services list.
3. Rollback steps with clear commands or runbook references.
4. Monitoring dashboards and metric thresholds.
5. Communication plan (internal and customer-facing if relevant).

Contact and ownership matrix:
- Policy curator: Priya Natarajan (finance-legal)
- Access & Security escalation: Marta Alvarez (Security)
- Procurement lead: Evan Chen (Finance)
- Change CAB coordinator: Liam O'Connor (Eng-Platform)

Revision history:
- 2025-10-15: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added data retention defaults and travel threshold updates (reviewers: Evan Chen, Marta Alvarez).
- 2026-02-01: Published as company policy and linked canonical templates.

Related reading and links:
- Company Ops Index (search: company-operations-reference-2025)
- Security: Risk & Exceptions playbook

End of document.

Overview

Purpose: This document consolidates company-wide process patterns and canonical templates that operational teams (finance, security, people-ops, engineering, and product) should follow when executing cross-functional activities: access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the internal templates used to standardize execution.

3. Instrumentation: Ensure tracing, profiling, and key metric dashboards are updated.
4. Run smoke tests and the regression prompt-set where model-serving behavior might change.
5. Perform progressive rollout using feature flags and monitor error budget and latency SLOs for 30 minutes after each step.
6. If any threshold breach occurs, trigger automatic rollback and page the on-call.

Escalation thresholds (example):
- Latency increase > 20% from baseline for 5 minutes -> immediate rollback.
- Error rate > 1% for 3 minutes -> rollback + incident review.

Change windows: Standard business hours preferred; Major changes require notified maintenance windows and customer-facing communications.

-----

3) Data Management (classification, retention, handling)

Principles: Minimize retained PII, apply defense-in-depth, and document lineage for datasets used for training/eval.

Classification tiers:
- Public: Documentation, marketing assets.
- Internal: Product specs, runbooks, non-PII operational data.
- Restricted: Customer data, secrets, regulated datasets.

Retention and Access Rules (summary table in plain text):
| Data Type | Retention Default | Access Pattern | Owner |
| Customer logs (non-PII) | 180 days | Read for support & SRE | Customer Success |
| Customer PII | 90 days (default) | Minimal, audited | Security |
| Model training datasets | 365 days (review) | Restricted; request-based | Applied ML Team |
| Audit logs | 2 years | Security & Compliance | Security |

Data Handling Requirements:
- All Restricted data must be stored in approved repositories (VPC-only buckets, customer-dedicated storage) and encrypted at rest with KMS keys.
- Masking/pseudonymization is required before using customer telemetry for model training unless explicit consent/contractual terms allow.
- Data deletion requests must be tracked via the Data Deletion Request template and completed within contractual timelines.

Lineage and Provenance:
- Every dataset used for model onboarding must include a provenance document: source, transformation steps, retention policy, and contact owner.

-----

4) Procurement & Expenses

Objective: Streamline purchases and expense reimbursement while providing delegation controls and budget visibility.

Purchase thresholds and authority matrix:
- <= $5,000: Team manager approval.
- $5,001–$50,000: Department head approval + procurement review.
- > $50,000: VP-level approval + legal/compliance review.

Approved procurement flow:
1. Create a Purchase Request (PR) using the Purchase Request template with business justification, budget code, and vendor info.
2. Procurement verifies vendor due diligence checklist (insurance, security posture questionnaire if needed).
3. Procurement creates PO and routes to vendor.
4. Finance records the commit against the budget and tracks payments.

Expense policy highlights:
- Reimbursable items: travel, client entertainment (with receipts and business justification), approved training.
- Non-reimbursable: personal expenses, fines, or items without documentation.
- Expense submission: within 30 days of expenditure; late submissions require manager exception.

Travel approvals:
- Any trip > $2,500 requires explicit manager and travel coordinator approval and a travel safety review for international travel.

-----

5) Vendor Management

Vendor lifecycle stages: Selection -> Contracting -> Onboarding -> Monitoring -> Offboarding

Minimum due diligence checklist (selection stage):
- Legal & security review (standard clause checklist).
- SOC2 or equivalent evidence for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Financial health check for long-term commitments.

Onboarding artifacts required:
- Signed contract with SLAs and termination rights.
- Security questionnaire and remediation plan.
- Contact and escalation list (vendor-side and Redwood-side).

Monitoring cadence:
- Strategic vendors: quarterly business reviews (QBRs).
- Operational vendors: monthly KPI review and incident syncs as needed.

Offboarding checklist:
- Data return or certified deletion; revoke credentials and ensure accounts terminated.
- Final invoice reconciliation and retention of contract for 7 years (per legal retention schedule).

-----

6) Travel & Event Policy (high-level)

Principles: Safety first, cost-consciousness second, equitable access to travel for required business needs.

Booking rules:
- Use approved travel portal; economy class for flights under 5 hours, premium economy or business only with pre-approved manager exception.
- Hotel selection must use corporate rates when available.

Safety & Approvals:
- International travel requires manager approval and travel safety check for high-risk countries.
- Emergency travel assistance details and insurance coverage are available through People Ops.

Expenses and per diems:
- Meal per diem caps differ by region; itemize expenses and provide receipts for amounts over $75 USD equivalent.

Conference sponsorships and exhibitor booths:
- Additional procurement flow required for significant spend on booths or sponsorships. See Procurement section.

-----

7) Internal Templates and Checklists (where to find them)

Canonical templates (use and link names):
- access-request-template (Access & Permissions)
- change-request-template (Change Management)
- procurement-purchase-request (Procurement & Expenses)
- vendor-onboarding-pack (Vendor Management)
- data-provenance-checklist (Data Management)
- travel-approval-form (Travel)
- post-incident-review (Incident Response)

Template usage rules:
- Teams must not create local forks of templates without documenting the differences and obtaining an owner-team signoff.
- All templates have a metadata header that includes template owner, last revision date, and related policy link.

-----

Governance and Exceptions

Exception process:
1. Document the exception request using the Exception Request template, including risk assessment and mitigation.
2. Route to owner_team and governance board (depending on risk tier).
3. Approval requires a documented compensating control and an expiration date.

Periodic review cycle:
- Policy and templates review cadence is annual. High-risk controls (access, data handling) are reviewed semi-annually.

Metrics and KPIs (suggested):
- Time-to-provision (median hours) for access requests.
- Change success rate (percentage of changes with no rollback).
- Vendor security compliance coverage (%) for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Expense processing time (median days).

Appendix: Example change request checklist (text)
1. Change summary and owner contact.
2. Impact analysis and affected services list.
3. Rollback steps with clear commands or runbook references.
4. Monitoring dashboards and metric thresholds.
5. Communication plan (internal and customer-facing if relevant).

Contact and ownership matrix:
- Policy curator: Priya Natarajan (finance-legal)
- Access & Security escalation: Marta Alvarez (Security)
- Procurement lead: Evan Chen (Finance)
- Change CAB coordinator: Liam O'Connor (Eng-Platform)

Revision history:
- 2025-10-15: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added data retention defaults and travel threshold updates (reviewers: Evan Chen, Marta Alvarez).
- 2026-02-01: Published as company policy and linked canonical templates.

Related reading and links:
- Company Ops Index (search: company-operations-reference-2025)
- Security: Risk & Exceptions playbook

End of document.

source.title: Entitlement Orchestration and Expense Standards
source.author: Aisha Raman
source.content:
Overview:

This document defines a company-wide, cross-functional standard for entitlement (access) lifecycle orchestration, change control for production services, data classification and retention expectations, procurement and expense approval thresholds, vendor onboarding baseline, and travel spend allowances. It combines policy with operational runbooks and templates so teams can consistently and audibly execute low-risk, high-compliance processes.

Scope:
- Applies to all Redwood Inference employees, contractors, and third-party vendors that touch Redwood systems, data, or financial processes.
- Includes cloud and on-prem resources, SaaS subscriptions, P-card and purchases, travel bookings, and vendor contracts.
- Does not replace team-level runbooks that add stricter controls; those must reference this standard and explain deviations.

Principles:
1. Least privilege by default: every entitlement must have a documented business justification and periodic attestation.
2. Separation of duties: approval chains must not converge on single-seat decision makers where conflicts exist.
3. Transparency and auditability: decisions and evidence are retained for a minimum of retention windows specified below.
4. Risk-based controls: higher sensitivity data and higher-dollar procurement require stronger checks (security review, legal, finance).

Roles and responsibilities:
- Requester: Employee or contractor submitting an access, procurement, or travel request.
- Approver: Designated manager or system owner whose sign-off is required.
- Data Owner: Product or platform owner accountable for classification and retention decisions.
- Security Owner: InfoSec representative who signs off on external vendor access or sensitive changes.
- Procurement/Finance: Validates budgets, tax, contract terms, and issues PO or processes P-card reconciliation.
- People Ops: Responsible for entitlement lifecycle at onboarding and offboarding.

1) Access and permissions lifecycle

Overview:
All entitlements (console/API keys, role bindings, database credentials, SaaS admin accounts) must flow through the Entitlement Orchestration process. The goal is to provide fast access while ensuring reviewability and automated revocation on role change or departure.

Process (high level):
1. Request: Use the Access Portal (internal URL: https://confluence.internal/access-portal placeholder) to submit a request with justification, least-privilege scope, and optional expiry.
2. Approval: Automated routing to the designated Approver and Data Owner. Requests for elevated privileges require Security Owner approval.
3. Provisioning: Approved requests are executed via automation (Terraform, IAM automation) where possible; manual provisioning must be recorded in the ticket with timestamp and actor.
4. Attestation: Quarterly attestation emails for managers to confirm active entitlements.
5. Revocation: Automatic on termination; otherwise requester/manager/owner can request revocation. Emergency revocation follows the Incident process (see section 4).

Entitlement tiers and controls:
| Tier | Typical resources | Approval chain | Max expiry (auto) | Additional controls |
|------|-------------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Tier 1 (Broad) | Organization admin, billing | Manager + Security + Finance (if billing) | 7 days | Requires 2FA + hardware token for long-lived keys |
| Tier 2 (Privileged) | Production deploy, DB admin | Manager + Data Owner + Security | 30 days | Justification required; MFA enforced; usage logging enabled |
| Tier 3 (Scoped) | Service accounts, feature flags | Manager | 90 days | Scoped least-privilege roles; IAM policies reviewed on change |

Temporary access patterns:
- Emergency (SEV-high): 1-hour temporary elevation using the break-glass tool; audit log required and postmortem within 48 hours.
- Contractor access: max 90 days, tied to contract, reviewed by People Ops weekly.

2) Change management (services and infra)

Purpose:
Ensure production changes are safe, reversible, and observable; reduce blast radius while enabling engineering velocity.

Change categories:
- Standard: low-risk, pre-authorized (e.g., documentation config changes). No change window required; automated rollout allowed with health gates.
- Minor: code or infra changes with routine risk. Requires change ticket, peer review, CI green, and can be deployed within standard windows.
- Major: schema changes, network reconfiguration, data migrations. Requires change advisory board (CAB) sign-off, pre-deploy verification, runbook, and rollback plan.

Pre-change checklist (required for Minor and Major):
1. Change ticket created (JIRA or internal tracker) linking PR and CI artifacts.
2. Owner and on-call assigned for the change window.
3. Rollback plan documented with commands and thresholds.
4. Monitoring and alerting hooks verified (tracing, metrics, log retention).
5. Stakeholders notified 48 hours in advance for Major changes.

Change windows and SLOs:
- Standard day changes: 06:00–20:00 local business hours where SRE is available. Night/EOD changes require explicit on-call overlap.
- Roll forward/rollback target: < 15 minutes to revert for service-level config changes; < 2 hours for data migrations (may vary by scope).

Emergency change flow:
1. Change marked emergency in ticketing system with SEV tag.
2. On-call executes approved break-glass step or escalation to Service Owner.
3. Post-change: 72-hour incident review with RCA if impact > 0.5% error budget.

3) Data management and classification

Classification levels:
- PUBLIC: No restrictions; may be indexed by search engines.
- INTERNAL: Company information intended for employees and contractors; do not publish externally.
- CONFIDENTIAL: Business-sensitive data (non-PII financials, roadmaps); access on need-to-know basis.
- RESTRICTED: PII, regulated data, customer secrets, or data governed by contractual/legislative requirements.

Retention baseline (by data type):
- Audit logs: 365 days (hot), archive 3 years cold.
- Backups: 90 days rolling snapshot; 7-year archival for financial records as required by Finance & Legal.
- User PII: retention per contract; default retention 2 years unless otherwise required.
- Model training artifacts: follow Data Owner policy; sensitive training data must be tagged and isolated.

Handling rules for RESTRICTED data:
- Encryption at rest and in transit (TLS 1.2+).
- Access limited to Tier 2 or higher with explicit Data Owner approval.
- Vendor storage for Restricted data requires DPA, SOC 2 Type II or equivalent, and signed SDS controls.

Data deletion and legal holds:
- Legal or compliance can place a legal hold, pausing deletion and retention cycles.
- Deletion requests must go through Data Owner and People Ops (where eDiscovery/records are involved).

4) Procurement and expense flows

Approval thresholds and routing:
- <$2,500: P-Card or team manager approval. Goods/services must be budgeted.
- $2,500–$25,000: Procurement ticket required; manager + Procurement review. Requires three vendor bids for new categories where feasible.
- $25,000–$150,000: Procurement + Finance + Legal review; Procurement issues PO; contract required for multi-year or recurring spend.
- >$150,000: Enterprise procurement + Legal + Executive sponsor; board notification for multi-year commitments over $1M.

Procurement procedure (step-by-step):
1. Create Procurement Request (internal form) with business justification, budget code, vendor details, and estimated total cost.
2. Procurement validates vendor (insurance, tax ID, COI) and checks existing contracts.
3. For new vendors: security assessment + DPA negotiation as required by data classification.
4. Approvals routed to manager and Procurement/Finance; Finance verifies budget and issues PO.
5. Vendor is onboarded (vendor onboarding checklist) and SLAs stored in Vendor Vault.

Expense submission (employee reimbursements):
- Timeline: Submit within 30 days of transaction. Exceptions require manager approval.
- Receipts: Itemized receipts required for all expenses > $25. Card statements alone are insufficient.
- Reimbursement window: Finance processes monthly; aim for reimbursement within 45 days of submission.

Per diem and travel booking guidance (summary):
- Domestic (US): $75/day meal allowance; actual lodging reimbursed with receipts up to $250/night for major metros; mid-tier caps apply for small metros.
- Europe: €85/day meal allowance; lodging cap €220/night for major cities.
- APAC: regional cap, typically equivalent to USD $200/night for major metros.
- Pre-approval: Any trip with estimated total > $1,000 requires manager sign-off and pre-trip form.
- Preferred booking: Use company travel portal or approved agency. Personal upgrades are allowed with out-of-pocket expense and manager sign-off.

5) Vendor onboarding and management

Baseline vendor checklist (must be completed before PO issuance for vendors handling company data):
1. Vendor profile created in Vendor Registry with primary contact, country of incorporation, tax ID.
2. Security questionnaire completed; SOC 2 or equivalent evidence provided for vendors storing or processing Restricted data.
3. DPA / contract template negotiation completed by Legal.
4. Insurance and indemnity verification by Procurement.
5. Onboarding runbook: credentials, authorized contacts, escalation paths, and access expiration dates recorded.

Vendor risk tiers and monitoring:
- Low: commodity SaaS (no customer data). Quarterly inventory review.
- Medium: analytical services, limited customer data. Quarterly security check-ins and annual contract reviews.
- High: vendors with access to customer data or production systems. Monthly operational review and continuous monitoring where feasible.

Renewals and termination:
- Renewal reminders: Procurement triggers 90/60/30-day reminders.
- Offboarding: revoke credentials, terminate integrations, confirm data deletion per DPA.
- Exit audit: where required, Vendor must provide certification of data destruction and handover artifacts.

6) Templates and checklists (operational appendix)

A. Access request template (fields):
- Requester name, email, team
- Resource requested (ARN, hostname, SaaS URL)
- Scope (read/write/admin)
- Business justification and duration
- Data classification impacted

B. Procurement request template (fields):
- Requester, department, budget code
- Vendor legal name, contact, tax ID
- Estimated amount, recurring period, justification
- Security classification requirement (Public/Internal/Confidential/Restricted)

C. Change lifecycle checklist (to include in JIRA ticket):
- PR link, test plan, rollback commands, metrics to monitor (error rate, latency, saturation), owner on-call contact, post-deploy verification steps.

D. Expense submission checklist:
- Expense category, date, merchant, amount, currency, receipt attachment, cost center, trip or event reference, manager approval, GL code.

Appendix: example automation snippets

Sample IAM request payload (YAML):
---
requester: "alice@redwood.ai"
resource: "projects/prod-cluster/namespaces/analytics/serviceAccount:etl-worker"
scope: "roles/viewer"
justification: "Temporary access to debug nightly job"
expiry: "2025-03-31T23:59:59Z"
---

Procurement Jira template summary (text):
- Summary: Purchase: Snowflake compute (Q3)
- Description: Business justification, expected ROI, list of owners
- Attachments: Security questionnaire, draft SOW

Operational controls and evidence retention:
- All approvals and evidence are stored in the central Records store (linked from ticket).
- Audit trails for entitlement changes retained 365 days in hot storage and archived to cold storage for 3 additional years.

Deviations and exceptions:
- Teams may request permanent exceptions via the Exceptions Board (submit via internal form). Each exception must include compensating controls, defined duration (max 12 months), and owner. Exceptions are reviewed quarterly.

Governance and reviews:
- Quarterly policy review by Finance & Legal + Security + People Ops to ensure thresholds and retention windows remain fit-for-purpose.
- Annual tabletop (game-day) exercise covering emergency revocation, vendor breach scenario, and procurement fraud scenario.

Contacts and escalation:
- Procurement queue: procurement@redwood.ai
- Security incidents and vendor security questions: infosec@redwood.ai
- People Ops for onboarding/offboarding entitlements: peopleops@redwood.ai
- Finance reimbursements: expenses@redwood.ai

Revision history:
- 2025-02-12: Initial draft and cross-team consultation.
- 2025-08-05: Added vendor risk tiers and extended log retention guidance.
- 2026-01-15: Updated procurement thresholds and travel per diem caps; published.

Related artifacts and references:
- Vendor Vault (internal link placeholder): /confluence/vendor-vault
- Access Portal SOP: /confluence/access-portal-sop
- Change Advisory Board charter: /confluence/cab-charter

End of document.
