source.title: Operational Catalog: Access Lifecycle, Change Controls, Data Stewardship, and Supplier Onboarding
source.author: Nina Patel
source.body:
Purpose:
This document consolidates company-wide operational policies and repeatable procedures governing: access and permissions, change management, data stewardship, procurement & expenses, vendor onboarding, travel, and the standard templates teams must use. It is intended as the canonical cross-functional catalog for engineering, product, security, finance, and people-ops.

Scope:
Applies to all Redwood Inference employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to company systems or data. Exceptions must be approved by Finance-Legal and Security.

Summary of Key Principles:
- Least privilege: grant the minimum access required for the role and timeboxed when possible.
- Separation of duties: requests that change financial or production states must involve at least two independent approvers.
- Auditability: every privilege change, production change, procurement, and vendor onboarding event must create an auditable ticket with owner, justification, and artifacts.
- Risk-based controls: controls scale with data sensitivity and financial exposure.

Owners and Contacts:
- Catalog owner: Nina Patel (Finance-Legal) — nina.patel@redwood.ai
- Access governance: Security (team alias: security@redwood.ai) — primary contact: Marcus Liu
- Change management operations: Eng Platform (eng-platform@redwood.ai) — primary contact: Sofia Romero
- Procurement & vendor coordination: Finance (procure@redwood.ai) — primary contact: Ethan Park

1) Access & Permissions
Overview:
Access lifecycle is managed through the Access Request System (ARS), group-managed identity (GMI) roles, and short-lived elevated sessions (just-in-time). All non-public systems require an ARS ticket ID embedded in the access grant.

Roles and Responsibilities:
- Requester: provides business justification, scope, and duration.
- Approver: manager-level for standard access; system owner for high-sensitivity assets.
- Access Review Owner: a designated manager or system owner who runs quarterly reviews.

Access Categories and Approval Matrix:
| Access Level | Example Systems | Approval Required | Max Duration |
|---|---|---:|---:|
| Standard employee | Slack, Google Drive (non-sensitive folders) | Manager | 1 year |
| Elevated dev | Internal staging clusters, debug consoles | Manager + System Owner | 30 days |
| Production operator | Prod serving runtime, deployment API | System Owner + SRE on-call | 7 days |
| Sensitive data access | PII datasets, audit logs | Data Steward + Security | 14 days |
Request Flow (standard):
1. Create ARS ticket with justification and target resources.
2. Manager approves (auto-notify system owner if required).
3. Access is provisioned via GMI and an audit log entry is created.
4. Ticket must be tagged with a re-review date and auto-closure if timeboxed.

Emergency access (break-glass):
- Use Break-Glass form (link in ARS) and notify Security + SRE immediately.
- All break-glass grants are time-limited to 4 hours and must be converted to formal ARS request within 24 hours with post-incident justification.

Access Review and Remediation:
- Quarterly entitlement reviews for all groups.
- Automated orphan account detection runs weekly; remediation tickets assigned to the account owner with 7-day SLA.

Sample Access Request Template (to paste into ARS):
```
Title: Access request — [system] — [purpose]
Requester: [name]
Justification: [business need, minimal scope]
Target resources: [resource identifiers]
Duration: [days]
Approvers: [manager email, system owner]
```

2) Change Management
Purpose:
Ensure predictable, observable, and reversible changes to production and critical systems while minimizing customer-facing disruptions.

Change Categories:
- Standard (pre-approved low risk): routine config changes, small infra tweaks.
- Normal: requires review and approval via Change Request (CR).
- Emergency: immediate fixes for P1/P0 incidents; follow-up CR required.

Change Request Process (Normal changes):
1. Submit CR in the Change Tracker with impact assessment, rollback plan, test cases, deployment window, and monitoring plan.
2. Engineering change owner schedules review with affected service owners.
3. CAB review required for changes with: cross-team impact, data migrations, vendor contracts, or >$50k monthly spend impact.
4. After approval, schedule deployment within allowed windows (see Deployment Windows).
5. Post-deploy verification (smoke tests) and CR close-out within 48 hours.

Required Artifacts for every Normal CR:
- CR form with owners and stakeholders.
- Runbook snippet for deployment and rollback.
- Test checklist and automated test links.
- Observability hooks (dashboards/alerts) to verify success.

Deployment Windows and Service Hours:
- Low-risk infra: Mon–Thu 10:00–18:00 PT.
- High-risk / customer-facing services: default weekend or off-peak windows agreed with SRE.
- Emergency changes allowed outside windows with on-call approval.

Rollback Plan Template (short form):
```
Change ID: CR-########
Rollback owner: [name]
Trigger(s) for rollback: [error rates, latency, failed smoke]
Estimated rollback time: [minutes]
Rollback steps: [commands/steps]
Verification after rollback: [checks]
```

Change KPIs and SLAs:
- CR review SLA: 3 business days for Normal changes.
- Emergency follow-up CR: create within 24 hours.
- Post-deploy verification: complete within 48 hours.

3) Data Management and Classification
Data Classification Levels:
- Public: safe for external distribution.
- Internal: company-internal operational data.
- Confidential: business-sensitive data (contracts, customer metadata).
- Restricted: PII, PHI, regulated data requiring enhanced controls.

Handling Requirements by Class:
- Encryption at rest and in transit required for Confidential and Restricted.
- Access controls: RBAC + just-in-time for Restricted.
- Logging: all Restricted data access must emit fine-grained audit events retained 2 years.
- Data minimization: services must not store Restricted data unless approved by Data Steward.

Retention Policy:
- Default retention for Internal data: 2 years.
- Confidential: 3–7 years depending on contract/legal requirements.
- Restricted: retention determined by legal/data steward; default 7 years.

Data Lifecycle Responsibilities:
- Data Stewards: designate classification, retention, and approved consumers.
- Platform teams: enforce technical controls (encryption, logging).
- Security: periodic audits and policy exceptions handling.

Data Incident Handling:
- Report data incidents to security@redwood.ai immediately.
- Triage, impact assessment, and notification per incident response policy.

4) Procurement, Expenses & Travel
Procurement Thresholds and Approvals:
| Amount (USD) | Approval Path |
|---:|---|
| <$1,000 | Requester Manager |
| $1,000–$10,000 | Manager + Finance reviewer |
| $10,000–$75,000 | Director + Procurement |
| >$75,000 | VP Finance + Legal sign-off |
Purchase Order (PO) Process:
1. Create PR in Procure system with vendor, scope, and estimated cost.
2. Finance validates budget and cost center.
3. Procurement issues PO and assigns vendor onboarding task.

Expense Reimbursement and Travel:
- Use Expense system within 30 days of expenditure.
- Standard per diem and travel class: domestic economy, international economy for <8h flights; business allowed with VP approval.
- Receipts required for all transactions >$25.
- Travel risk assessments required for high-risk geographies (see Travel Advisory grid link).

Company Card and Delegation:
- Department-level cards for recurring subscriptions must be owned by a manager.
- Card holder responsibility includes monthly reconciliation within 7 days of statement.

5) Vendor Management and Onboarding
Vendor Risk Tiers:
- Tier 1 (Strategic/High risk): hosts customer data, manages production services. Requires Security review, SOC2 + contract addenda.
- Tier 2 (Moderate risk): business tools with access to internal systems. Requires DPIA and standard contract.
- Tier 3 (Low risk): marketing suppliers, consultants. Standard MSA acceptable.

Onboarding Checklist (minimum):
1. Vendor profile in Vendor Registry (legal name, tax id, primary contact).
2. Risk assessment completed by Procurement + Security.
3. Contract with data processing addendum if applicable.
4. Approved purchase order and payment terms.
5. Technical onboarding and least-privilege integration plan.
6. Regular review cadence defined (quarterly for Tier 1, annually for Tier 2).

Termination and Offboarding:
- Ensure deprovisioning checklist executes: revoke keys, remove service accounts, terminate access, confirm data disposition or return per contract.
- Financial closeout and final invoice reconciliation required within 30 days.

6) Templates & Required Forms (links to systems are referenced by name)
- Access Request (ARS): use the ARS form. Include resource IDs and expiry.
- Change Request (CR): use Change Tracker CR template. Attach rollbackplan and tests.
- Vendor Intake: Vendor Registry form plus procurement PR.
- Expense form: Expense system standard template; attach receipts.

Example Change Request (brief):
```
Title: CR — Reduce KV cache retention for model-X
Owner: Priya Desai
Impact: Possible short-term increase in cold-start latency for 1–2 minutes
Rollback: Re-enable previous retention config
Tests: end-to-end inference smoke + load test in staging
Window: Wed 02:00–04:00 PT
```

7) Audit, Metrics, and Compliance
Key Metrics (sample targets):
- Access request median completion: 48 hours (standard), 4 hours (elevated)
- % of orphan accounts remediated within 7 days: 95%
- CR review SLA adherence: 95% within 3 business days
- Vendor onboarding complete within target: Tier 1 — 30 days, Tier 2 — 15 days

Audit & Review Cadence:
- Quarterly operational audit covering access reviews, CR closeouts, vendor compliance.
- Annual external vendor security attestation review for Tier 1 vendors.

Exceptions and Policy Changes:
- Exceptions require documented risk acceptance signed by Finance-Legal and Security.
- Policy changes follow a review period of 2 weeks with notification to company-wide channels and update of this catalog.

Appendices
A. Contacts & Escalation Matrix:
- Security escalation: security@redwood.ai -> on-call security lead -> CISO
- Procure escalation: procure@redwood.ai -> head of procurement -> VP Finance
- Production change escalation: eng-platform@redwood.ai -> SRE lead -> CTO

B. Quick Reference: Common Forms and Links:
- ARS (Access Request System): https://tools.redwood.ai/ars
- Change Tracker: https://tools.redwood.ai/change-tracker
- Vendor Registry: https://tools.redwood.ai/vendor-registry
- Expense Portal: https://tools.redwood.ai/expenses

C. Revision History:
- 2024-11-12: Document created (Nina Patel)
- 2025-02-07: Updated procurement thresholds and emergency access windows (reviewers: Marcus Liu, Sofia Romero)

If you have questions about how these policies apply to your project, open a ticket to finance-legal@redwood.ai and include the system names and proposed exceptions.

title: Operational Catalog: Access Lifecycle, Change Controls, Data Stewardship, and Supplier Onboarding
preview:
Purpose:
This document consolidates company-wide operational policies and repeatable procedures governing: access and permissions, change management, data stewardship, procurement & expenses, vendor onboarding, travel, and the standard templates teams must use. It is intended as the canonical cross-functional catalog for engineering, product, security, finance, and people-ops.

Scope:
Applies to all Redwood Inference employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to company systems or data. Exceptions must be approved by Finance-Legal and Security.

Summary of Key Principles:
- Least privilege: grant the minimum access required for the role and timeboxed when possible.
- Separation of duties: requests that change financial or production states must involve at least two independent approvers.
- Auditability: every privilege change, production change, procurement, and vendor onboarding event must create an auditable ticket with owner, justification, and artifacts.
- Risk-based controls: controls scale with data sensitivity and financial exposure.

Owners and Contacts:
- Catalog owner: Nina Patel (Finance-Legal) — nina.patel@redwood.ai
- Access governance: Security (team alias: security@redwood.ai) — primary contact: Marcus Liu
- Change management operations: Eng Platform (eng-platform@redwood.ai) — primary contact: Sofia Romero
- Procurement & vendor coordination: Finance (procure@redwood.ai) — primary contact: Ethan Park

1) Access & Permissions
Overview:
Access lifecycle is managed through the Access Request System (ARS), group-managed identity (GMI) roles, and short-lived elevated sessions (just-in-time). All non-public systems require an ARS ticket ID embedded in the access grant.

Roles and Responsibilities:
- Requester: provides business justification, scope, and duration.
- Approver: manager-level for standard access; system owner for high-sensitivity assets.
- Access Review Owner: a designated manager or system owner who runs quarterly reviews.

Access Categories and Approval Matrix:
| Access Level | Example Systems | Approval Required | Max Duration |
|---|---|---:|---:|
| Standard employee | Slack, Google Drive (non-sensitive folders) | Manager | 1 year |
| Elevated dev | Internal staging clusters, debug consoles | Manager + System Owner | 30 days |
| Production operator | Prod serving runtime, deployment API | System Owner + SRE on-call | 7 days |
| Sensitive data access | PII datasets, audit logs | Data Steward + Security | 14 days |
Request Flow (standard):
1. Create ARS ticket with justification and target resources.
2. Manager approves (auto-notify system owner if required).
3. Access is provisioned via GMI and an audit log entry is created.
4. Ticket must be tagged with a re-review date and auto-closure if timeboxed.

Emergency access (break-glass):
- Use Break-Glass form (link in ARS) and notify Security + SRE immediately.
- All break-glass grants are time-limited to 4 hours and must be converted to formal ARS request within 24 hours with post-incident justification.

Access Review and Remediation:
- Quarterly entitlement reviews for all groups.
- Automated orphan account detection runs weekly; remediation tickets assigned to the account owner with 7-day SLA.

Sample Access Request Template (to paste into ARS):
```
Title: Access request — [system] — [purpose]
Requester: [name]
Justification: [business need, minimal scope]
Target resources: [resource identifiers]
Duration: [days]
Approvers: [manager email, system owner]
```

2) Change Management
Purpose:
Ensure predictable, observable, and reversible changes to production and critical systems while minimizing customer-facing disruptions.

Change Categories:
- Standard (pre-approved low risk): routine config changes, small infra tweaks.
- Normal: requires review and approval via Change Request (CR).
- Emergency: immediate fixes for P1/P0 incidents; follow-up CR required.

Change Request Process (Normal changes):
1. Submit CR in the Change Tracker with impact assessment, rollback plan, test cases, deployment window, and monitoring plan.
2. Engineering change owner schedules review with affected service owners.
3. CAB review required for changes with: cross-team impact, data migrations, vendor contracts, or >$50k monthly spend impact.
4. After approval, schedule deployment within allowed windows (see Deployment Windows).
5. Post-deploy verification (smoke tests) and CR close-out within 48 hours.

Required Artifacts for every Normal CR:
- CR form with owners and stakeholders.
- Runbook snippet for deployment and rollback.
- Test checklist and automated test links.
- Observability hooks (dashboards/alerts) to verify success.

Deployment Windows and Service Hours:
- Low-risk infra: Mon–Thu 10:00–18:00 PT.
- High-risk / customer-facing services: default weekend or off-peak windows agreed with SRE.
- Emergency changes allowed outside windows with on-call approval.

Rollback Plan Template (short form):
```
Change ID: CR-########
Rollback owner: [name]
Trigger(s) for rollback: [error rates, latency, failed smoke]
Estimated rollback time: [minutes]
Rollback steps: [commands/steps]
Verification after rollback: [checks]
```

Change KPIs and SLAs:
- CR review SLA: 3 business days for Normal changes.
- Emergency follow-up CR: create within 24 hours.
- Post-deploy verification: complete within 48 hours.

3) Data Management and Classification
Data Classification Levels:
- Public: safe for external distribution.
- Internal: company-internal operational data.
- Confidential: business-sensitive data (contracts, customer metadata).
- Restricted: PII, PHI, regulated data requiring enhanced controls.

Handling Requirements by Class:
- Encryption at rest and in transit required for Confidential and Restricted.
- Access controls: RBAC + just-in-time for Restricted.
- Logging: all Restricted data access must emit fine-grained audit events retained 2 years.
- Data minimization: services must not store Restricted data unless approved by Data Steward.

Retention Policy:
- Default retention for Internal data: 2 years.
- Confidential: 3–7 years depending on contract/legal requirements.
- Restricted: retention determined by legal/data steward; default 7 years.

Data Lifecycle Responsibilities:
- Data Stewards: designate classification, retention, and approved consumers.
- Platform teams: enforce technical controls (encryption, logging).
- Security: periodic audits and policy exceptions handling.

Data Incident Handling:
- Report data incidents to security@redwood.ai immediately.
- Triage, impact assessment, and notification per incident response policy.

4) Procurement, Expenses & Travel
Procurement Thresholds and Approvals:
| Amount (USD) | Approval Path |
|---:|---|
| <$1,000 | Requester Manager |
| $1,000–$10,000 | Manager + Finance reviewer |
| $10,000–$75,000 | Director + Procurement |
| >$75,000 | VP Finance + Legal sign-off |
Purchase Order (PO) Process:
1. Create PR in Procure system with vendor, scope, and estimated cost.
2. Finance validates budget and cost center.
3. Procurement issues PO and assigns vendor onboarding task.

Expense Reimbursement and Travel:
- Use Expense system within 30 days of expenditure.
- Standard per diem and travel class: domestic economy, international economy for <8h flights; business allowed with VP approval.
- Receipts required for all transactions >$25.
- Travel risk assessments required for high-risk geographies (see Travel Advisory grid link).

Company Card and Delegation:
- Department-level cards for recurring subscriptions must be owned by a manager.
- Card holder responsibility includes monthly reconciliation within 7 days of statement.

5) Vendor Management and Onboarding
Vendor Risk Tiers:
- Tier 1 (Strategic/High risk): hosts customer data, manages production services. Requires Security review, SOC2 + contract addenda.
- Tier 2 (Moderate risk): business tools with access to internal systems. Requires DPIA and standard contract.
- Tier 3 (Low risk): marketing suppliers, consultants. Standard MSA acceptable.

Onboarding Checklist (minimum):
1. Vendor profile in Vendor Registry (legal name, tax id, primary contact).
2. Risk assessment completed by Procurement + Security.
3. Contract with data processing addendum if applicable.
4. Approved purchase order and payment terms.
5. Technical onboarding and least-privilege integration plan.
6. Regular review cadence defined (quarterly for Tier 1, annually for Tier 2).

Termination and Offboarding:
- Ensure deprovisioning checklist executes: revoke keys, remove service accounts, terminate access, confirm data disposition or return per contract.
- Financial closeout and final invoice reconciliation required within 30 days.

6) Templates & Required Forms (links to systems are referenced by name)
- Access Request (ARS): use the ARS form. Include resource IDs and expiry.
- Change Request (CR): use Change Tracker CR template. Attach rollbackplan and tests.
- Vendor Intake: Vendor Registry form plus procurement PR.
- Expense form: Expense system standard template; attach receipts.

Example Change Request (brief):
```
Title: CR — Reduce KV cache retention for model-X
Owner: Priya Desai
Impact: Possible short-term increase in cold-start latency for 1–2 minutes
Rollback: Re-enable previous retention config
Tests: end-to-end inference smoke + load test in staging
Window: Wed 02:00–04:00 PT
```

7) Audit, Metrics, and Compliance
Key Metrics (sample targets):
- Access request median completion: 48 hours (standard), 4 hours (elevated)
- % of orphan accounts remediated within 7 days: 95%
- CR review SLA adherence: 95% within 3 business days
- Vendor onboarding complete within target: Tier 1 — 30 days, Tier 2 — 15 days

Audit & Review Cadence:
- Quarterly operational audit covering access reviews, CR closeouts, vendor compliance.
- Annual external vendor security attestation review for Tier 1 vendors.

Exceptions and Policy Changes:
- Exceptions require documented risk acceptance signed by Finance-Legal and Security.
- Policy changes follow a review period of 2 weeks with notification to company-wide channels and update of this catalog.

Appendices
A. Contacts & Escalation Matrix:
- Security escalation: security@redwood.ai -> on-call security lead -> CISO
- Procure escalation: procure@redwood.ai -> head of procurement -> VP Finance
- Production change escalation: eng-platform@redwood.ai -> SRE lead -> CTO

B. Quick Reference: Common Forms and Links:
- ARS (Access Request System): https://tools.redwood.ai/ars
- Change Tracker: https://tools.redwood.ai/change-tracker
- Vendor Registry: https://tools.redwood.ai/vendor-registry
- Expense Portal: https://tools.redwood.ai/expenses

C. Revision History:
- 2024-11-12: Document created (Nina Patel)

source.title: Production Change Management Standard (v2)
source.author: Aisha Rahman
source.content:
h1. Purpose
This policy defines what constitutes a production change at Redwood Inference and the minimum requirements to plan, approve, execute, and validate the change. The goal is to minimize customer impact while maintaining developer velocity.

h1. Scope
Applies to all production environments for the Hosted API and Console, including:
* Hosted API control plane and data plane services
* Console UI and backend
* Shared platform dependencies (routing, auth, billing metering, observability agents)

Out of scope: customer-managed environments for *Private deployments* (see the Private Deployment Change Policy), and vendor-managed changes outside Redwood’s control (cloud provider maintenance).

h1. Definitions
* *Production:* Any environment serving external customer traffic (including canary and regional production).
* *Production change:* Any change that can affect confidentiality, integrity, availability, latency, cost, or correctness for customer-facing workloads.
* *Change owner:* The person accountable for end-to-end execution and follow-through.
* *Approver:* A designated peer/manager/SRE/security reviewer depending on risk tier.
* *Change record:* A durable record (ticket + links) capturing intent, risk, approvals, execution, and validation results.

h1. Change types
|| Change type || When used || Required approvals || Notes ||
| Standard | Low risk, pre-approved pattern (e.g., doc-only, internal dashboard query fixes, non-prod config) | 1 peer approval | Must match an approved standard template |
| Normal | Most code/config deploys to production | 1 peer approval + (1) service owner approval | Requires pre-deploy verification, canary, and comms assessment |
| Emergency | Active incident mitigation or preventing imminent customer impact | On-call incident commander (IC) + service owner approval (as available) | Retrospective change record required within 24 hours |

h1. What counts as a production change (non-exhaustive)
* Any deployment to production clusters (including canary regions)
* Any change to routing policies, fallback policies, or model selection rules
* Any change to auth, RBAC, API keys, rate limits, quotas, or billing meters
* Any schema migration, backfill, or data retention configuration change
* Any infrastructure change that affects capacity, autoscaling, or failover
* Any change to observability pipelines that could reduce visibility (logs, metrics, tracing)

h1. Minimum required artifacts
For every production change, the change owner must create a change record that includes:
* Summary of change and customer impact assessment
* Rollback plan (explicitly stated, even if “re-deploy previous build”)
* Verification plan (how we will know it worked)
* Links to: PR(s), build(s), dashboards, runbook used, and any migration plan
* Approvals (captured in the change record)

h1. Risk assessment checklist
Answer these before requesting approval:
1. Could this change affect auth, data paths, encryption, logging, or retention?
2. Could this change change request routing, batching, caching, or model version selection?
3. Could this change materially change token accounting/billing?
4. Is there any data migration/backfill involved?
5. Is rollback fast and safe (< 10 minutes) and does it revert the customer experience?

If “yes” to any of 1–4, the change is *Normal* at minimum and may require Security Review (see Secure SDLC requirements).

h1. Approvals
* *Peer approval:* A reviewer from the same engineering org who is familiar with the subsystem.
* *Service owner approval:* The tech lead or on-call owner for the service being changed.
* *SRE gate:* Required when the change affects capacity, SLO-sensitive paths, traffic routing, or any failover config.
* *Security gate:* Required when the change touches auth, secrets, encryption, audit logging, PII, or data retention.

h1. Change windows and freezes
* Default window: weekdays 09:00–16:00 local time for the primary owning team.
* Changes are prohibited during declared change freezes.
* Additional restrictions apply when error budgets are low (see “Change Freeze and Risk Assessment”).

h1. Execution requirements
All production changes must:
1. Use the documented runbook for the target system (Hosted API or Console).
2. Perform a canary rollout for traffic-bearing services unless explicitly exempted in the change record.
3. Confirm observability before and after (golden signals and specific dashboards).
4. Have a clearly defined rollback trigger and a “stop the line” principle.

h1. Communications requirements
Change owner must determine if customer communications are required:
* *Planned maintenance:* Status page notice and direct customer comms if any material impact is expected.
* *No expected customer impact:* Internal announcement in #eng-releases and on-call awareness if during core hours.
See “Planned Maintenance and Change Communications” for templates and required lead time.

h1. Post-change validation and closure
Within 60 minutes after completion, the change owner must:
* Validate key SLO metrics and error rates are within normal bounds
* Confirm canary is healthy before full rollout
* Update the change record with results and attach screenshots/links
* File follow-ups for any issues discovered

h1. References
* Prod Deployment Gates (Hosted API + Console)
* Hosted API Deploy Runbook
* Console Deploy Runbook
* Rollback and Post-change Validation
* Change Freeze and Risk Assessment
* Secure SDLC: Security Review Requirements for Prod Changes
* Planned Maintenance and Change Communications

title: Production Change Management Standard (v2)
preview:
h1. Purpose
This policy defines what constitutes a production change at Redwood Inference and the minimum requirements to plan, approve, execute, and validate the change. The goal is to minimize customer impact while maintaining developer velocity.

h1. Scope
Applies to all production environments for the Hosted API and Console, including:
* Hosted API control plane and data plane services
* Console UI and backend
* Shared platform dependencies (routing, auth, billing metering, observability agents)

Out of scope: customer-managed environments for *Private deployments* (see the Private Deployment Change Policy), and vendor-managed changes outside Redwood’s control (cloud provider maintenance).

h1. Definitions
* *Production:* Any environment serving external customer traffic (including canary and regional production).
* *Production change:* Any change that can affect confidentiality, integrity, availability, latency, cost, or correctness for customer-facing workloads.
* *Change owner:* The person accountable for end-to-end execution and follow-through.
* *Approver:* A designated peer/manager/SRE/security reviewer depending on risk tier.
* *Change record:* A durable record (ticket + links) capturing intent, risk, approvals, execution, and validation results.

h1. Change types
|| Change type || When used || Required approvals || Notes ||
| Standard | Low risk, pre-approved pattern (e.g., doc-only, internal dashboard query fixes, non-prod config) | 1 peer approval | Must match an approved standard template |
| Normal | Most code/config deploys to production | 1 peer approval + (1) service owner approval | Requires pre-deploy verification, canary, and comms assessment |
| Emergency | Active incident mitigation or preventing imminent customer impact | On-call incident commander (IC) + service owner approval (as available) | Retrospective change record required within 24 hours |

h1. What counts as a production change (non-exhaustive)
* Any deployment to production clusters (including canary regions)
* Any change to routing policies, fallback policies, or model selection rules
* Any change to auth, RBAC, API keys, rate limits, quotas, or billing meters
* Any schema migration, backfill, or data retention configuration change
* Any infrastructure change that affects capacity, autoscaling, or failover
* Any change to observability pipelines that could reduce visibility (logs, metrics, tracing)

h1. Minimum required artifacts
For every production change, the change owner must create a change record that includes:
* Summary of change and customer impact assessment
* Rollback plan (explicitly stated, even if “re-deploy previous build”)
* Verification plan (how we will know it worked)
* Links to: PR(s), build(s), dashboards, runbook used, and any migration plan
* Approvals (captured in the change record)

h1. Risk assessment checklist
Answer these before requesting approval:
1. Could this change affect auth, data paths, encryption, logging, or retention?
2. Could this change change request routing, batching, caching, or model version selection?
3. Could this change materially change token accounting/billing?
4. Is there any data migration/backfill involved?
5. Is rollback fast and safe (< 10 minutes) and does it revert the customer experience?

If “yes” to any of 1–4, the change is *Normal* at minimum and may require Security Review (see Secure SDLC requirements).

h1. Approvals
* *Peer approval:* A reviewer from the same engineering org who is familiar with the subsystem.
* *Service owner approval:* The tech lead or on-call owner for the service being changed.
* *SRE gate:* Required when the change affects capacity, SLO-sensitive paths, traffic routing, or any failover config.
* *Security gate:* Required when the change touches auth, secrets, encryption, audit logging, PII, or data retention.

h1. Change windows and freezes
* Default window: weekdays 09:00–16:00 local time for the primary owning team.
* Changes are prohibited during declared change freezes.
* Additional restrictions apply when error budgets are low (see “Change Freeze and Risk Assessment”).

h1. Execution requirements
All production changes must:
1. Use the documented runbook for the target system (Hosted API or Console).
2. Perform a canary rollout for traffic-bearing services unless explicitly exempted in the change record.
3. Confirm observability before and after (golden signals and specific dashboards).
4. Have a clearly defined rollback trigger and a “stop the line” principle.

h1. Communications requirements
Change owner must determine if customer communications are required:
* *Planned maintenance:* Status page notice and direct customer comms if any material impact is expected.
* *No expected customer impact:* Internal announcement in #eng-releases and on-call awareness if during core hours.
See “Planned Maintenance and Change Communications” for templates and required lead time.

h1. Post-change validation and closure
Within 60 minutes after completion, the change owner must:
* Validate key SLO metrics and error rates are within normal bounds
* Confirm canary is healthy before full rollout
* Update the change record with results and attach screenshots/links
* File follow-ups for any issues discovered

h1. References
* Prod Deployment Gates (Hosted API + Console)
* Hosted API Deploy Runbook
* Console Deploy Runbook
* Rollback and Post-change Validation
* Change Freeze and Risk Assessment
* Secure SDLC: Security Review Requirements for Prod Changes
* Planned Maintenance and Change Communications

source.title: Emergency change policy (production)
source.author: Naomi Feldman
source.content:
h1. Policy statement
An *emergency change* is a production change required to mitigate an active or imminent customer-impacting incident. Emergency changes are allowed to bypass standard change windows and review timelines, but must meet the approval and documentation requirements below.

h1. Examples
* Emergency rollback of a Serving Runtime release
* Disabling a faulty feature flag causing elevated 5xx
* Routing traffic away from a degraded region

h1. Minimum required approvals
Emergency changes require at least one of:
* Incident Commander approval (for an active incident), OR
* SRE oncall approval (if no IC assigned yet)

Additional required approvals:
* If the change impacts *Dedicated* customers with premium SLAs: CS oncall or Account Owner must be notified as soon as practical.
* If the change touches authentication, RBAC, audit logging, or encryption: Security oncall must approve.

h1. Documentation requirements
Within 24 hours of the emergency change:
* Create or update the incident record with:
  * what was changed
  * when it was changed
  * who approved
  * verification results
* Link the CI/CD job(s) and dashboards used to validate recovery

h1. Post-change review
* SEV1/SEV2 emergency changes require a postmortem
* During the postmortem, document why standard change management was bypassed and what guardrails will prevent repeat issues

h1. Interaction with releases
If an emergency change is a rollback:
* Pause progressive delivery
* Pin the release tag
* Lock auto-advance until the incident is resolved

h1. References
* Incident SEV process
* Runtime rollback runbook
* Runtime release pipeline rollback procedure
